title: Add structured tool-contract playground and inline snippet runner
preview:
Context and motivation:
This PR introduces a compact, interactive playground in the docs site for authoring and validating structured tool contracts used by RAG agents. Our goal is to lower the onboarding time for integrators and to make it trivial to iterate on tool schemas and example snippets while seeing live telemetry for invocation patterns. The playground targets: engineers prototyping tool contracts, solutions engineers demonstrating integrations, and docs authors writing reproducible examples.

What this change contains (single-string summary):
- New docs page at /playgrounds/structured-tool-contract with an embedded snippet runner that executes sample contracts in a sandboxed iframe and shows structured validation feedback.
- Snippet runner JS (site/static/js/snippet-runner.js) implements a short-lived local shim that validates contract JSON and emits telemetry probes to telemetry/probes/tool-contract-playground-probes.yaml.
- Two example tool contracts in examples/tool-contracts/ with annotated comments and a tiny README showing best practices (edge cases for optional params, param typing, and permission hints).
- CI addition to run a lightweight Puppeteer smoke job that loads the playground and asserts the validation pane renders.

Checklist:
- [x] Docs page with inline examples and schema reference
- [x] Example contracts and README
- [x] Snippet runner with basic sandbox validation and UX affordances
- [x] Telemetry probes to help us instrument sample usage patterns
- [x] CI smoke test to reduce regressions

How to run locally (developer notes):
1) yarn; yarn build:docs; yarn start:docs-local
2) Open http://localhost:3000/playgrounds/structured-tool-contract
3) Use the left panel to paste or load example-tool-contract.json; hit Run to validate and simulate a call.
4) Telemetry events are emitted to the local dev logger (see README section) or to the telemetry dev endpoint when using a configured key.

Key design decisions and rationale:
- We keep validation deterministic and side-effect free: the snippet runner does not call external endpoints by default; it simulates responses according to the contract spec so users can iterate without credentials.
- Telemetry probes are intentionally coarse-grained (invocations, validation_errors, snippet_exec_duration_ms) to avoid PII and to keep event volume low.
- The runner is implemented as a minimal JS shim (no backend) so the docs site remains self-contained and deployable to static hosting.

Testing and CI notes:
- CI initially showed a flaky Puppeteer job (timeout on CI step 'playground-render'), I relaxed the timeout and re-ran; final status: pass.
- Unit tests added for JSON schema validation logic (examples/tool-contracts/validation.test.js).

Review conversation (high-level extract):
- Diego Morales: "Nice UX; can we show an explicit schema diff when the runner auto-fixes an obvious typing issue?"
- Ava Chen (author): "Added a compact 'auto-fix suggestions' panel and a small note that fixes are opt-in; included in latest patch."
- Priya Nair: "Telemetry looks reasonable, please add sampling on the exec_duration metric."
- Ava Chen: "Added 10% sampling and documented it in probes yaml (commented)."
- Luca Bianchi: "CI flake observed; recommends increasing Puppeteer timeout and adding a retry. Approved after fixes."

Merge outcome and follow-ups:
- Merged using squash/merge on 2026-03-07 after approvals from Priya and Luca and successful CI.
- Follow-up work: ENG-4821: wire production telemetry ingestion pipeline to support playground signals; DOC-99: expand the playground with a multi-tool scenario and live mock responses.

Files changed (high-level snippets):
- docs/playgrounds/structured-tool-contract.md: includes the embedded runner iframe and a short walkthrough with code samples. Example snippet: `{"name":"lookup_customer","params":{"customer_id":"string"}}`
- site/static/js/snippet-runner.js: ~220 lines implementing schema validation, simulated response generation, and a small event emitter to surface telemetry.
- examples/tool-contracts/sample-tool-contract.json: two contract examples (sync lookup and async streaming notify).
- telemetry/probes/tool-contract-playground-probes.yaml: defines event names and sampling rules (comments included).

Notes for maintainers:
- This is docs-first and non-breaking. If you want to iterate on UX, update the static JS and test via the local docs server.
- If telemetry sampling is changed, please coordinate with the platform telemetry team to avoid sudden ingest spikes.

If there are any additional reviewers we should ping (security for telemetry, or platform for ingestion), I can open follow-up tasks. Thanks to the reviewers for quick turnarounds and to SRE for helping stabilize the CI job.

title: Multi-locale RAG quickstart and i18n evaluation playbook
Add compact authoring checkpoints and telemetry-guided RAG templates
preview: Motivation: customers asked for smaller, repeatable authoring artifacts that capture stable checkpoints for RAG authoring workflows and enable telemetry-driven regressions during prompt/index changes. This PR adds a compact 'checkpoint' template format, new telemetry-guided assertion examples, and an embedded authoring devcontainer/watch-mode recipe so engineers can iterate locally against seeded indexes. What this includes: 1) new compact checkpoint schema and examples (YAML+JSON) that capture prompt seeds, index snapshot pointers, and golden-response assertions; 2) a telemetry-guided assertions guide that maps telemetry signals (token-latency, rerank-quality, recall-window) to test thresholds and suggested remediation; 3) two new runnable samples: typescript/mini-agent and python/authoring-worker that demonstrate checkpoint-driven test runs and automatic rollback hooks; 4) a minimal devcontainer and a lightweight file-watcher that automatically runs the checkpoint harness on file changes; 5) an authoring VS Code extension manifest (samples only) that surfaces checkpoint failures inline and links to telemetry dashboards. Checklist: - [x] Add checkpoint schema and examples - [x] Add step-by-step guide for telemetry assertions - [x] Add TS and Python samples with README how-to - [x] Add devcontainer + watch-mode recipe - [x] Add tests for the harness smoke flow - [x] Docs lint and build passed How to validate: follow the README in docs/rag-authoring/checkpoints to run the devcontainer, create a checkpoint, and run `python harness.py --checkpoint ./samples/checkpoints/minimal.yaml`. Related work: follows ENG-8945 (authoring stability) and references the RAG telemetry taxonomy doc. Migration notes: existing RAG tutorials are unaffected; checkpoint templates are opt-in. This PR is documentation and samples only; no runtime changes to production serving are included.

title: Add RAG synthetic corpus authoring lab and docs
preview:
Motivation: teams building Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows often lack reproducible, small-scale corpora and a compact authoring loop to iterate on indexing/chunking/labeling. This PR introduces a focused "authoring lab" in the docs site: a guided walkthrough that teaches how to (1) synthesize representative corpora from heterogeneous sources, (2) apply chunking and metadata strategies, (3) run local smoke-evals against a typed prompt set, and (4) surface telemetry to detect dataset drift. The authoring lab contains conceptual guidance, a step-by-step tutorial, runnable examples (Python and TypeScript), and a tiny browser-hosted runner for manual experimentation.

What changed (high level): added a new docs page at /docs/rag/authoring/synthetic-corpora-lab with a compact tutorial, two language examples under docs/rag/authoring/examples, a portable JS runner in site/static/playgrounds to demo sampler behavior in the browser, an image diagram, and a CI smoke workflow to exercise the examples on PRs. The doc includes a short checklist for productionizing synthetic corpora (legal/PII review, provenance tags, sampling seeds). The tutorial is lightweight and intended to be copy-paste friendly for product engineers and platform teams.

Checklist: - [x] Draft tutorial text and diagrams - [x] Add Python example (notebook + small script) - [x] Add TypeScript example (ts + run script) - [x] Add browser runner demo - [x] Add CI smoke pipeline to run basic examples - [x] Add telemetry probes guide and sample metrics queries - [x] Run lint and docs build locally

Testing and CI: the new CI job (ci/smoke/synth-corpus-smoke.yml) runs a small matrix: python example smoke test, ts build + node run, and a docs build to ensure the new page compiles. CI passed in the last run. I validated the python notebook outputs locally and exercised the browser-runner in Chrome. The smoke tests are intentionally very small (a few synthetic documents) so they are cheap and fast.

Docs UX notes: the page is authored as a hands-on lab with collapsible code blocks and small executable snippets. Code samples are written to be copy-paste runnable with minimal dependencies (requests/openai-client placeholders, local LightIndexer shim). The page also references the existing "RAG authoring checklist" and links back to the retrieval tuning guide.

Backward compatibility / breaking changes: none. This is documentation and examples only; there are no runtime or API changes.

Related: ENG-4921 (improve retrieval authoring flows), DOC-1285 (docs lab initiative), SUP-3210 (customer request for reproducible corpora guidance).

title: Document Optimize quality gates, results interpretation, and override workflow
preview: This PR adds first-pass internal/external documentation for Optimize “quality guardrails gating” to ensure cost-saving configuration recommendations (batching, caching, quantization profiles) cannot be applied to production traffic without passing quality evaluation gates (or a time-bound, auditable override). The docs target both self-serve users (what you’ll see in Console, how to interpret pass/fail) and enterprise/security reviewers (audit trail, RBAC, override controls). Sections included: (1) Overview: what quality gates are and what changes they apply to (batching policy updates, prefix/KV caching settings, quantization profile switches). (2) Gate lifecycle: Proposed → Gates pending/running → Passed/Failed → Apply allowed/blocked → Rollout canary coupling. (3) Results interpretation: metrics shown, baseline selection, how to read deltas, common failure reasons (sample size, flakiness, task success regression, rubric regression). (4) Overrides: who can approve, how to request, required evidence, expiration, and how it appears in audit logs; includes warning copy for UI parity. (5) Operational guidance: what to do when a gate fails (rerun vs investigate), and pointers to runbooks and dashboards. (6) Known limitations for limited rollout (hosted API first; Dedicated defaults next; Private workflow manual) and expected timeline. This PR does not change product behavior; it documents the behavior implemented in redwood + eval-harness + observability-pack PRs referenced below.

title: Console: Usage Insights docs (route/model breakdown, cost drivers, exports, troubleshooting)
preview:
## Summary
Adds the initial help-center / docs-site page for **In-console Usage Insights (Beta)**. The doc explains how the route+model breakdown is computed, defines the key metrics (tokens in/out, cache, retries/fallbacks, streaming overhead), and includes a troubleshooting section based on early beta/support feedback. This is intended to ship with the Console beta rollout and will be linked from the Usage Insights pages.

## What’s included
- New doc page: **Console → Usage → Usage Insights (Beta)**
- Definitions and attribution semantics (aligned with platform ADR/API contract)
- Guidance on interpreting cost drivers and “What changed?” deltas
- Export (CSV) overview + permissions/audit expectations
- Troubleshooting / known issues (billing reconciliation, missing route tags, time range timeouts)
- Screenshot placeholders with callouts (to be replaced once final UI polish lands)

## Notes on terminology
We use **Route** in the Console UI and docs to mean the canonicalized request path + method as recorded by the gateway (e.g., `POST /v1/chat/completions`). Where customers expect “endpoint,” we call out that Route ≈ Endpoint and explain normalization (path params templated).

## Security/RBAC
Docs explicitly call out that cost visibility and exporting are gated by workspace settings and role permissions. Also notes that export/download events are audit-logged (field set referenced in INT-9140).

## Screenshot placeholders
Added `TODO(screenshots)` blocks with suggested capture specs:
- Workspace selector + time range
- Breakdown table (Route × Model) with sorting
- Drilldown view with context length histogram and cache hit rate
- Top Cost Drivers card(s)
- Export flow modal + permission denied state

## Related work
- Console UI: redwood/pr-23141
- Export endpoints + signed downloads: redwood/pr-23148
- RBAC gating: redwood/pr-23160
- Data freshness banner: redwood/pr-23170

## Checklist
- [x] Align metric definitions with Console tooltips (DevEx feedback)
- [x] Include billing reconciliation caveats and expected tolerances
- [x] Add troubleshooting entries for known beta issues (SUP-24811 / SUP-24827 / SUP-24863)
- [x] Security review callout for audit logs + export permissions
- [x] Mark as Beta + document limitations

## Open items (follow-ups)
- Replace placeholders with final screenshots after UI copy freeze (target: before beta announcement post)
- Add a short “How to export via API” snippet once examples repo script is public (redwood-examples/pr-622)
- Expand section on model aliases/version bucketing once ENG-3823 fully lands (avoid duplicate-row confusion)

title: Docs: audit logging event types + export destinations (SIEM/S3) with config examples
preview:
Motivation: Sales/SEs have been repeatedly asked for a crisp, copy/paste-ready explanation of Redwood audit logging (what events exist, what fields are included, how to export, and what limitations/retention knobs exist by deployment mode). The existing docs were too high-level and didn’t include enough concrete configuration examples, which created drift between RFP language and what customers can actually implement.

What’s in this PR: (1) A new “Audit log event taxonomy” section with stable event names and a minimal field contract (so customers can build detections/ingestions without relying on internal-only fields). (2) An “Export destinations” section covering supported patterns used in the field today: pull via API, push/ship to customer-owned S3-compatible object storage, and SIEM ingestion via customer-managed forwarder (Splunk/Elastic/etc.). (3) Practical configuration examples (curl + SDK snippets) and a troubleshooting appendix that Solutions Engineering can point to during security reviews.

Scope notes / non-goals: This is documentation-only. It intentionally does not promise roadmap (e.g., ‘native’ direct integrations to specific SIEM vendors). Where behavior differs between Hosted, Dedicated, and Private, the docs call this out explicitly and link to the Security/Compliance enablement materials for evidence request process.

Customer-facing highlights: - Clear list of audit event types: auth/admin actions, API key lifecycle, org/user management, model deployment/rollout actions, data retention setting changes, and export configuration changes. - Export overview: API-based export (cursor pagination) and object storage export (customer bucket; customer-owned keys; Redwood writes). - Examples for common workflows: export last 24h to S3, consume via Athena/Glue, and forward into SIEM via a customer-owned collector.

Screenshots/diagrams: No screenshots. Includes a small ASCII data-flow diagram for export paths.

Related: ENG-13988 (add docs/examples for audit log export integrations referenced by enablement kit) and PM-4821 (Enterprise Security Enablement Kit refresh).

title: Document tiered SLOs + error budget policy (Hosted vs Dedicated) and escalation expectations
preview: Summary: This PR updates internal and external documentation to reflect Redwood’s tiered reliability commitments for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved capacity) and the associated error budget policy. It explains (a) canonical SLIs and SLO targets at a high level, (b) how to interpret error budget status in Console and what it means operationally, and (c) escalation expectations and what data is required for support/SRE triage. Motivation: We’ve had repeated customer escalations around latency spikes and 5xx/stream disconnects during burst traffic, plus confusion about what counts against SLOs (client aborts, invalid requests, rate-limits, fallback/retries). This PR consolidates and standardizes wording across docs site pages used by CS/SE and customers. Scope: Hosted + Dedicated only; Private is mentioned as future work and explicitly out-of-scope for enforcement in this phase. Contents included in this PR: Tiered SLO overview page with definitions (SLI vs SLO vs SLA), tier scope, and in-scope endpoints (chat/completions, embeddings, streaming). Error budget explainer page: reporting window, what “budget remaining” means, burn-rate alerting concept (multi-window), and common misunderstandings (e.g., a short spike can be OK if it doesn’t materially burn budget). Streaming reliability section: what counts as a streaming failure, how we distinguish server disconnects vs client aborts, and how retries/fallback are accounted for. Escalation guide: for Hosted vs Dedicated, expected response path, when an event becomes an incident, and the required intake fields (timestamps, region, model, request IDs, and whether it was streaming). Console guidance: where to find the SLO dashboards, how to filter by tier/region, and how to interpret annotations (deploys/incidents). Non-goals: This PR does not change SLO targets themselves, does not publish contractual SLA language, and does not add new product UI—only docs updates and alignment. Notes on terminology: We intentionally use “SLOs (internal)” vs “SLAs (contractual)” and call out that exclusions may differ between internal measurement and contract language. title: SDK docs: add streaming troubleshooting (timeouts, reconnect, idempotency) + cross-links to known issues
preview:
## Summary
Adds a dedicated streaming troubleshooting section to the SDK docs and links it consistently to the new Known Issues hub articles. The goal is to reduce Support back-and-forth by giving customers copy/paste-ready guidance for the most common streaming failure modes (timeouts, reconnect, truncated streams) and by standardizing our language around safe retries and idempotency.

This PR is part of the Customer Success & Support escalation playbook refresh / known-issues sprint work. It is intentionally customer-safe (no account names, no internal dashboards).

## What’s included
- New docs page: “Troubleshooting streaming” with:
  - Common symptoms + likely causes (proxy idle timeouts, client read timeouts, network path resets)
  - Recommended client timeout defaults (connect vs read) and keepalive guidance
  - Reconnect flow for SSE/streaming responses (retry with jittered backoff)
  - Safe retry rules + idempotency guidance (what is safe to retry and what isn’t)
  - Clear “When to escalate to Support” criteria + required info (request IDs, region, model, SDK version)
- Cross-links to Known Issues:
  - Known issue: Streaming timeouts and reconnect (workarounds)
  - Known issues index landing page
- Minor edits to Python/TS/Go streaming guides to point to the troubleshooting page (instead of repeating partial guidance).

## Rationale
Support tickets SUP-1842 / SUP-1854 (and multiple repeats) consistently stall on:
- Unclear separation of customer network/proxy timeouts vs Redwood-side disconnects
- Inconsistent retry advice (some customers retry mid-stream unsafely)
- Missing “what to send Support” context (request IDs, regions, model/version, whether HTTP/2 is in play)

This doc consolidates the guidance and keeps the workaround details in the Known Issues pages so Support can update mitigations without chasing SDK guide copies.

## Not included / follow-ups
- No changes to server behavior; see ENG-4822 (improve streaming disconnect error details) for runtime/gateway improvements.
- No customer-specific mitigations beyond what’s already published as a Known Issue.

## Screenshots
- N/A (text-only docs).

## Implementation notes
- The troubleshooting content lives under SDK docs so it’s discoverable from language-specific guides.
- The “When to escalate” section aligns with the playbook language (request IDs, time window, error strings, impacted region/model).

### Example snippet (docs)
"If you see `stream reset` / truncated output mid-generation: (1) retry the request from the beginning (do not attempt to resume partial tokens), (2) use exponential backoff with jitter, and (3) include the `x-request-id` from the failed attempt when contacting Support. If failures persist for >10 minutes in a single region or affect multiple regions, escalate per the Known Issue guidance."

## Checklist
- [x] Links verified (no relative-link rot)
- [x] Terminology aligned: “stream reset / truncated stream / disconnect”
- [x] Support-safe (no internal tooling references)
- [x] Reviewed by Support/SRE for accuracy on retry + escalation criteria

Related:
- PR #2841 (Known issues hub + initial workaround articles)
- PR #2842 (Support docs nav updates + escalation/SLA links)

title: Internal docs: Cost Model API usage, field semantics, and troubleshooting
preview:
This PR updates the internal docs site to reflect Cost Model API v1 as the canonical source for cost primitives used by Console and Optimize. It adds concrete request/response examples, defines field semantics (esp. prefill vs decode, cache attribution, batch efficiency, and overhead allocation), and includes troubleshooting guidance pulled from early integrations and support escalations.

Motivation
- We’ve had multiple definitions of “cost” across telemetry, billing hooks, Console, and Optimize prototypes. Teams need a single, versioned set of docs that matches the schema package + service behavior.
- Customer-facing escalations (SUP-31844, SUP-31877) showed that missing/ambiguous semantics (overhead vs request-attributable; optimistic what-if assumptions) cause confusion and mis-triage.

What’s included
- New internal docs page: “Cost Model API (v1) – Usage & Semantics”
  - Endpoint overview: /v1/cost/estimate, /v1/cost/breakdown, /v1/cost/what-if
  - Version negotiation guidance (client sends supported_schema_versions; server responds with selected_schema_version)
  - Request bounding guidance for Console hot paths (time ranges, grouping cardinality, max dimensions)
- Field definitions aligned with ADR-022 taxonomy
  - Explicit definitions for prefill_token_count vs decode_token_count
  - cache_attributed_{prefill,decode}_tokens vs cache_hit_rate; how to interpret partial hits
  - batch_efficiency_ratio and queue_time_ms: what they represent and what they do NOT
  - overhead_allocation_policy_id + overhead_usd: how overhead is allocated and why invoice totals may differ
- What-if guardrails for Optimize
  - Allowed parameter ranges (batch size bounds, cache warm-rate bounds, quantization profile constraints)
  - Confidence interval fields and when to surface “low confidence” in the UI
- Troubleshooting section
  - “Console breakdown doesn’t match invoice” decision tree (most common causes: reporting window, overhead policy, telemetry lag, feature-flag fallback)
  - “What-if savings too optimistic” checklist (cache warm-rate assumptions, workload summary mismatch, quant profile incompatibility)
  - Common error codes and mitigations (SCHEMA_VERSION_UNSUPPORTED, REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED, TELEMETRY_LAGGING)

Notes
- Docs intentionally avoid duplicating the schema reference (generated from the schema package). This PR links to the canonical schema docs and adds human explanations + examples.
- All examples use fake org/project IDs and redacted model IDs.

Follow-ups
- Once Console feature flag rollout passes 50% traffic, add a short “Console integration notes” appendix with real-world latency numbers and caching recommendations (tracked in ENG-4825).

References
- ADR-021 Standardize cost model interfaces
- ADR-022 Cost breakdown taxonomy
- Cost Model API v1 design spec (internal Confluence)
- Support: SUP-31844, SUP-31877

title: Docs: Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics quickstart (enablement, interpretation, retention warnings)
preview:
Title: Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics (Beta) — Quickstart

What you’ll do in this guide
In this quickstart you’ll enable Shadow Traffic on a production route and compare “control” vs “shadow” variants using quality metrics—without changing what your users see. Shadow Traffic mirrors a sampled subset of real requests to one or more candidate variants (e.g., new model version, quantization profile, routing policy, or prompt template), scores the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surfaces quality deltas alongside latency/cost/error metrics.

Key properties (read this first)
- No user impact: The user always receives the control response. Shadowing is fail-open: if the shadow path fails, the control path continues unaffected.
- Asynchronous quality scoring: Quality metrics may lag behind request volume (ingestion + scoring queue). Plan for delayed dashboards.
- Beta behavior: Quality signals are advisory by default. Automatic rollback/quality gating depends on your rollout policy configuration and is off unless explicitly enabled.

Prerequisites
- You have access to the Redwood Console (or API) with permissions to manage routes/rollouts and view quality dashboards.
- Your route is already instrumented in Console (latency/cost/errors visible).
- You understand what you’re testing (model version vs quantization vs prompt template). Pick one variable per experiment when possible.

Concepts
- Control: The variant that serves user-visible responses.
- Shadow target: One or more variants that receive mirrored requests for evaluation only.
- Sampling rate: The fraction of production requests mirrored to the shadow target (e.g., 0.5%–5%).
- Deterministic bucketing: The same “type” of request is consistently assigned to the same shadow condition, reducing variance when comparing windows.
- Run: A continuous time window of shadowing under a fixed policy + rubric configuration.

Step 1 — Create a shadow experiment (Console)
1) Go to Console → Rollouts → Shadow Experiments → Create
2) Select a route (or an existing canary/A-B rollout object)
3) Choose your shadow target(s)
   - Example targets: model=rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@v2, quantization=int8-profile-b, or routing-policy=cost-optimized
4) Set sampling
   - Recommended defaults:
     - Start: 1% sampling
     - Increase: 2–5% once you confirm overhead and scoring lag are stable
   - If your traffic is low, use higher sampling to reach statistical power faster.
5) Set timeouts + fail-open behavior
   - Shadow timeout should be strict. If the shadow request times out, it’s recorded as shadow_error and the user response is unaffected.
6) Save

Step 2 — Enable shadowing via API (optional)
If you prefer automation (CI/CD or rollout pipelines), you can create and enable a shadow experiment via API.

Create experiment
curl -X POST https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"route_id":"rt_7w2k...","name":"llama70b-v2-shadow","sampling_rate":0.01,"shadow_targets":[{"type":"model","model_id":"rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@v2"}],"shadow_timeout_ms":2500,"fail_open":true,"rubric_version":"qm_v1.0","scorer_model_id":"rw/grader-13b@2026-02-01"}'

List runs (paginated)
curl -X GET "https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123/runs?limit=50&cursor=..." \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Disable shadowing quickly (kill switch)
If you need to stop shadowing due to overhead or an incident, disable the experiment (control traffic is unaffected either way):
curl -X POST https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123:disable \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Step 3 — Understand the default v1 quality metric bundle
Shadow Traffic ships with a default metric bundle (v1). Metrics are computed on shadow outputs and compared to control outputs for the same mirrored requests (paired comparison).

Metrics included in v1 (typical)
- Refusal rate: Fraction of responses classified as refusal / non-assistance.
- Format adherence: Whether the response matches the expected format (e.g., valid JSON schema, required keys, tool-call structure, or regex checks).
- Instruction-following rubric score: A model-graded rubric (0–5) measuring compliance with user instructions.
- Safety/refusal signals: Basic flags indicating safety policy triggers and refusal behaviors.

Important caveats
- Model-graded metrics can drift if the scorer model or rubric changes. Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id per experiment run.
- Heuristic checks (format/regex) are sensitive to prompt templates and can overcount failures if your format spec is ambiguous.

Step 4 — Interpret quality deltas (and confidence intervals)
Console → Shadow Experiments → (select experiment) → Results

You’ll see, per metric:
- Control value vs Shadow value
- Delta (Shadow - Control)
- Confidence interval (CI band)
- Sample size (N)

How to read this
- Look for deltas that are both practically meaningful and statistically stable. A small delta with a wide CI often means you need more samples.
- Prefer paired comparisons and keep the experiment configuration stable. Changing sampling, rubric, or target mid-run will complicate interpretation.
- Triangulate with latency/cost/error metrics. A “better” rubric score at the cost of large latency regressions may still be a net negative.

Recommended minimums (rule-of-thumb)
- For binary rates (refusal/format pass): aim for at least hundreds to thousands of paired samples before making a decision.
- For rubric scores: expect higher variance; you may need more samples or longer windows.

Scoring lag: why your dashboard may look “empty”
Shadow scoring is asynchronous. It’s normal to see request volume immediately but quality aggregates appear later. Use the “Scoring lag” panel to confirm whether you’re waiting on ingestion or scoring.

Step 5 — Drill into examples safely
Use “Examples” to inspect paired control vs shadow outputs for debugging regressions.

Security + privacy tips
- Do not rely on example views for long-term storage. Shadow artifacts are retained for a short window by default (see Retention & Redaction below).
- Avoid adding high-cardinality identifiers (prompt_id, user_id, email) as tags/labels. Prefer coarse segments (route, model, region, request_type, tenant_tier).

Retention & redaction (warnings and defaults)
Shadow Traffic can capture shadow artifacts (redacted and truncated by default) to support scoring and debugging.

Defaults (may vary by deployment / contract)
- Hosted API: short retention by default (typically 72 hours) for redacted artifacts; aggregates may be retained longer.
- Dedicated: configurable; common default is 7 days for redacted artifacts, longer for aggregates depending on plan.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): configurable and may default to minimal retention; storage location and export controls depend on your deployment.

What “redacted/truncated by default” means
- Sensitive fields may be removed, hashed, or replaced before storage.
- Prompt/response bodies may be truncated to limit exposure and cost.
- You can configure allowlists/redaction rules to keep only the fields necessary for scoring.

Access controls + auditability
- Shadow experiment creation/edits require route/rollout management permissions.
- Viewing examples may be restricted to elevated roles depending on tenant policy.
- Actions are recorded in audit logs (create/update/enable/disable), which can be exported for compliance workflows.

Cost and overhead guidance
- Shadow requests increase inference load on the shadow target. Start small (1%) and scale only after verifying capacity.
- Scoring workers also consume compute. If scoring lag grows, reduce sampling or metrics bundle size.
- Guardrails are designed to protect the user path. If you observe control-path latency impact, treat it as a P0 and disable shadowing.

Streaming requests (known constraints)
Shadowing streaming requests can capture partial outputs depending on timeout and stop conditions. If your use case relies on full-stream fidelity, verify behavior in a low sampling run first and use the Examples view to confirm.

Troubleshooting
Problem: “No quality deltas are showing up”
- Check scoring lag dashboard and pipeline health indicators.
- Confirm your sampling rate is non-zero and the experiment is enabled.
- Verify you’re not filtering by a segment with too few samples.

Problem: “Quality deltas look unstable / flip direction day-to-day”
- Increase sample size (or sampling rate) and keep the run configuration stable.
- Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id; avoid comparing across rubric changes.
- Ensure deterministic bucketing is enabled so request populations are consistent.

Problem: “Cardinality alerts or observability cost spike”
- Remove high-cardinality tags from experiment metadata.
- Use coarse segments and avoid emitting per-user identifiers.

Next steps
- Attach shadow experiment results to a canary rollout in Console for a unified timeline view.
- For prompt-set regression testing (offline), use eval-harness with pinned rubrics and compare against a known baseline.
- If you need stricter retention controls (regulated workloads), contact your Redwood account team to configure Dedicated/Private defaults and exports.

Links
- Rollouts overview
- Quality deltas and confidence intervals
- Shadow Traffic retention & redaction
- Shadow Traffic troubleshooting

- Heuristic checks (format/regex) are sensitive to prompt templates and can overcount failures if your format spec is ambiguous.

Step 4 — Interpret quality deltas (and confidence intervals)
Console → Shadow Experiments → (select experiment) → Results

You’ll see, per metric:
- Control value vs Shadow value
- Delta (Shadow - Control)
- Confidence interval (CI band)
- Sample size (N)

How to read this
- Look for deltas that are both practically meaningful and statistically stable. A small delta with a wide CI often means you need more samples.
- Prefer paired comparisons and keep the experiment configuration stable. Changing sampling, rubric, or target mid-run will complicate interpretation.
- Triangulate with latency/cost/error metrics. A “better” rubric score at the cost of large latency regressions may still be a net negative.

Recommended minimums (rule-of-thumb)
- For binary rates (refusal/format pass): aim for at least hundreds to thousands of paired samples before making a decision.
- For rubric scores: expect higher variance; you may need more samples or longer windows.

Scoring lag: why your dashboard may look “empty”
Shadow scoring is asynchronous. It’s normal to see request volume immediately but quality aggregates appear later. Use the “Scoring lag” panel to confirm whether you’re waiting on ingestion or scoring.

Step 5 — Drill into examples safely
Use “Examples” to inspect paired control vs shadow outputs for debugging regressions.

Security + privacy tips
- Do not rely on example views for long-term storage. Shadow artifacts are retained for a short window by default (see Retention & Redaction below).
- Avoid adding high-cardinality identifiers (prompt_id, user_id, email) as tags/labels. Prefer coarse segments (route, model, region, request_type, tenant_tier).

Retention & redaction (warnings and defaults)
Shadow Traffic can capture shadow artifacts (redacted and truncated by default) to support scoring and debugging.

Defaults (may vary by deployment / contract)
- Hosted API: short retention by default (typically 72 hours) for redacted artifacts; aggregates may be retained longer.
- Dedicated: configurable; common default is 7 days for redacted artifacts, longer for aggregates depending on plan.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): configurable and may default to minimal retention; storage location and export controls depend on your deployment.

What “redacted/truncated by default” means
- Sensitive fields may be removed, hashed, or replaced before storage.
- Prompt/response bodies may be truncated to limit exposure and cost.
- You can configure allowlists/redaction rules to keep only the fields necessary for scoring.

Access controls + auditability
- Shadow experiment creation/edits require route/rollout management permissions.
- Viewing examples may be restricted to elevated roles depending on tenant policy.
- Actions are recorded in audit logs (create/update/enable/disable), which can be exported for compliance workflows.

Cost and overhead guidance
- Shadow requests increase inference load on the shadow target. Start small (1%) and scale only after verifying capacity.
- Scoring workers also consume compute. If scoring lag grows, reduce sampling or metrics bundle size.
- Guardrails are designed to protect the user path. If you observe control-path latency impact, treat it as a P0 and disable shadowing.

Streaming requests (known constraints)
Shadowing streaming requests can capture partial outputs depending on timeout and stop conditions. If your use case relies on full-stream fidelity, verify behavior in a low sampling run first and use the Examples view to confirm.

Troubleshooting
Problem: “No quality deltas are showing up”
- Check scoring lag dashboard and pipeline health indicators.
- Confirm your sampling rate is non-zero and the experiment is enabled.
- Verify you’re not filtering by a segment with too few samples.

Problem: “Quality deltas look unstable / flip direction day-to-day”
- Increase sample size (or sampling rate) and keep the run configuration stable.
- Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id; avoid comparing across rubric changes.
- Ensure deterministic bucketing is enabled so request populations are consistent.

Problem: “Cardinality alerts or observability cost spike”
- Remove high-cardinality tags from experiment metadata.
- Use coarse segments and avoid emitting per-user identifiers.

Next steps
- Attach shadow experiment results to a canary rollout in Console for a unified timeline view.
- For prompt-set regression testing (offline), use eval-harness with pinned rubrics and compare against a known baseline.
- If you need stricter retention controls (regulated workloads), contact your Redwood account team to configure Dedicated/Private defaults and exports.

Links
- Rollouts overview
- Quality deltas and confidence intervals
- Shadow Traffic retention & redaction
- Shadow Traffic troubleshooting

(Internal notes for Redwood oncall: see runbook link in the SRE folder; not part of public docs.)

title: Publish marketplace security overview + buyer FAQ (AWS/GCP)
preview:
Goal: publish marketplace-friendly, customer-facing security docs that AWS/GCP reviewers and enterprise buyers can use without opening a ticket, while keeping claims accurate and consistent with our SOC 2/ISO-aligned controls. This PR adds (1) a Marketplace Security Overview page with a simple system/data-flow narrative and clear shared responsibility boundaries, and (2) a buyer-focused FAQ that answers common procurement/security questions (encryption, audit logs, retention, residency, incident response, support SLAs, and how to request evidence).

Key points / decisions (per Security & GRC review): we avoid absolute language like "we never store prompts" and instead document our default handling + configurable controls; we call out that some security evidence (SOC 2 Type II report, pen test summary) is available under NDA / upon request; we do not claim certifications we do not hold; we state encryption practices at a level appropriate for public docs (in transit + at rest, KMS options for Private) without disclosing sensitive implementation details.

Content added:
- /security/marketplace/overview: scope, deployment modes (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) and how security responsibilities differ, high-level data flow, encryption overview, identity/access, logging/audit, retention/residency, subprocessors pointers, and evidence request path.
- /security/marketplace/faq: short Q&A for procurement-led buyers, including marketplace subscription->entitlement->org provisioning expectations and who to contact when entitlement propagation is delayed.

Follow-ups (out of scope here): add diagrams once Design finalizes the screenshot/diagram pack (DES-913), and align FAQ wording with the final marketplace listing FAQ blocks once PMM locks copy for AWS/GCP submission.

title: Regional failover operator guide: automation behavior, runbook entry points, and pitfalls
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a new operator-facing guide for Redwood’s regional failover automation work (Reliability Program). The goal is to make the system understandable and operable for on-call: what the automation will do, what it will not do, where to start during an event, and how to validate outcomes.

The guide is intentionally written as an “operator manual” rather than a design doc: it prioritizes decision points, runbook entry points, and fast checks over implementation details.

## Why
During rehearsals and Game Day 1 we saw consistent confusion around:
- Which signals are authoritative vs corroborating (reachability vs 5xx burn vs tail latency vs capacity)
- When the controller is in dry-run vs execute mode and how to interpret recommendations
- Rollback and hold-down semantics (avoiding oscillation) and what on-call can safely override
- Where to look for the audit trail / incident timeline annotations (INT-1149)
- What “failover complete” means for Hosted vs Dedicated (including pool drain status)

This doc consolidates the above into a single entry point that links out to the existing runbooks and dashboards.

## What’s included
- New doc: `Regional failover automation: Operator guide`
  - Audience: SRE on-call, routing/control plane on-call, and incident commanders
  - Scope: Hosted API + Dedicated multi-region routing; *does not* cover Private bespoke automation
- A “Start here during an event” section with:
  - Triage flow: verify impairment → check region health summary → confirm corroborating signals → check controller mode → approve/monitor execution → validate and record
  - Direct links to runbooks (Hosted failover, controller runbook, GPU drain/evacuation)
- A “How automation decides” section:
  - Plain-language explanation of the trigger hierarchy and dampening/hold-down timers
  - What constitutes a region-level event vs partial degradation (ties to multi-region SLO definition)
- Guardrails and safety limits:
  - Per-tenant flip rate limits, blast radius constraints, and approvals in production
  - Rollback rules and anti-flap expectations (what on-call should *not* do)
- Observability + auditability:
  - What dashboards to open and what “good” looks like during failover
  - How to find controller audit events and incident-bot summaries
- Common pitfalls (from rehearsal notes and support questions):
  - False positives from partial network reachability loss
  - Latency spikes caused by client retries/reconnect patterns (esp. streaming)
  - Capacity headroom constraints in the target region and why failover can be intentionally gated
  - DNS TTL misconceptions (steering is not instant; caching + provider behavior)

## What’s not included
- No new product promises or SLA language (PM-512 tracks customer-facing UX/copy separately)
- No step-by-step duplication of existing runbooks; the guide links out to them and clarifies when to use each

## Docs structure changes
- Adds a Reliability/Operations section entry for “Regional failover automation”
- Adds cross-links and consistent terminology (region impairment, failover, rollback, hold-down, dry-run)

## Validation
- Ran docs build locally
- Link check (internal anchors + external Confluence/GitHub references)
- Confirmed the “Start here” flow matches current on-call ownership boundaries

## Follow-ups
- Once the failover controller CLI ships broadly, update the guide with stable command names and example outputs.
- After Game Day 2, fold in any additional pitfalls and update the success-criteria checklist.

Refs: ENG-2411 (umbrella), ENG-2423 (runbook refresh), PM-512 (customer-facing UX expectations), INT-1149 (timeline annotations), SUP-25405 (SLO definition question).

title: Add AWS/GCP Marketplace onboarding pages + entitlement troubleshooting
preview:
Summary: This PR adds first-class AWS and GCP Marketplace onboarding docs to reduce time-to-first-token for marketplace buyers and to unblock partner submission requirements. It introduces a Marketplace onboarding landing page, provider-specific step-by-step guides (Subscribe → Entitlement → Provision org → Create API key → First request), and an entitlement troubleshooting page with concrete error states and recommended next actions. It also links to the Marketplace Security Documentation Pack and clarifies which security artifacts are public vs available on request.

Motivation / context: Marketplace buyers are procurement-led and frequently get stuck during entitlement propagation and org linking. Partner feedback requested clearer customer-facing steps and explicit guidance for common failure modes (e.g., existing orgs, multi-subscription, delayed entitlement sync). This doc set is intended to pair with the console marketplace entrypoint (ENG-22112) and entitlement verification endpoint work (ENG-22110) by documenting the same error codes/UX copy.

What’s included: (1) New docs IA for Marketplace onboarding under /docs/marketplace/ with a landing page that routes customers by provider and deployment type (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). (2) AWS Marketplace guide covering subscription, private offers vs public offers, entitlement timing expectations, how to find product code / account identifiers needed for support, and first-call examples. (3) GCP Marketplace guide covering subscription, billing account prerequisites, procurement approvals, and first-call examples. (4) Troubleshooting: a dedicated Entitlements & provisioning troubleshooting page with a table mapping common errors (ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND, ENTITLEMENT_PENDING, ORG_ALREADY_LINKED, SUBSCRIPTION_MISMATCH, REGION_NOT_AVAILABLE) to remediation steps, including when to wait vs when to contact support and what data to provide. (5) Security: links to the Marketplace Security Documentation Pack index and a short ‘Security & compliance for marketplace buyers’ section with safe wording (no over-claims), plus notes on audit logs and retention controls.

Out of scope: Pricing details beyond high-level SKU descriptions (handled in the pricing/packaging pages update PR). No changes to console UX or backend entitlement logic.

Notes for partner submission: Final URLs introduced in this PR are stable and intended to be used in AWS/GCP marketplace listing fields. If redirects/vanity URLs are needed for older links, we should track separately (see INT-771).

QA: Ran local docs build, link checker, and spell/markdown lint. Verified nav placement and breadcrumbs. Confirmed that security links do not expose gated evidence directly and instead route to the security pack overview with ‘contact us’/‘on request’ language for reports.

Screenshots: Not included in this PR; screenshots will land once Design finalizes the capture set (DES-913).

title: Docs: Safe apply & rollback for Optimization Configs (Hosted/Dedicated)
preview: Summary: Adds a customer/admin-facing guide for versioned Optimization Configs, safe apply (validate → canary → promote), and one-click rollback for Hosted and Dedicated. Includes UX screenshots callouts (alt text only), API examples, and operational caveats (partial rollouts, cooldowns, metrics gating, and what rollback guarantees do/don’t mean). Motivation: Repeated customer asks for reversible optimization changes (batching/caching/quant profiles) and clear change-management/audit story (refs: SUP-18467, SUP-18421). This doc is intended to be linked from Console tooltips and the Optimize docs nav. Contents: 1) Concepts: config versions, scopes (tenant/model/region), active vs promoted version, and “known-good” baseline. 2) Safe apply workflow: validate, staged rollout with canary %, gating on SLOs (5xx + latency) and quantization quality signals, pause/resume semantics. 3) Rollback workflow: one-click rollback in Console + API endpoint, what happens in the serving fleet, expected propagation time, and post-rollback verification checklist. 4) Caveats/FAQs: partial rollout states, concurrent changes, cooldown windows, audit logs & RBAC permissions, and Dedicated-specific notes. Out of scope: Private deployment instructions (separate guide tracked elsewhere); automated rollback without human confirmation (early access). Screens/UX: Uses stable anchor links and avoids hardcoding UI strings that are still changing; includes short snippets for the confirmation modal copy and blast-radius summary. Validation: ran docs build locally; verified API examples with staging endpoints and ensured headers/fields align with current control plane naming (config_version, rollout_id).

title: Add Enterprise Security overview page (public) + SOC 2 request link stubs
preview: Context: Enterprise deals are increasingly gated on security/compliance review. We need a public, linkable Enterprise Security overview page that mirrors approved internal FAQ wording without leaking restricted evidence or promising roadmap items. This PR adds a new docs page with deployment-mode-specific notes (Hosted / Dedicated / Private), clarifies our customer data handling language, and includes stubs for requesting gated artifacts (e.g., SOC 2 Type II report) via the evidence request process. Scope/Non-goals: This is documentation-only and should not imply new product functionality; any features described must already exist. The page intentionally avoids: (a) claiming ISO 27001 certification, (b) stating absolute data residency guarantees without mode/region caveats, (c) offering pen-test reports publicly, and (d) describing future roadmap for audit logging/SCIM beyond current behavior. title: Private offline bundles: add FAQ + error code reference for signature verification failures
preview:
Summary
This PR adds a Private docs FAQ and a dedicated error code reference for offline bundle signature verification failures. The goal is to reduce time-to-triage for air-gapped installs by making installer failures actionable: what happened, why it happens, what to do next, and what to collect for Support.

What’s new
1) FAQ: Signed offline bundles
- What is being signed?
- Can we verify without installing?
- What does "fail closed" mean?
- How do key rotations work in air-gapped environments?
- What time sources are required?
- Can we use our own signing root? (Answer: not in initial GA; roadmap note only)

2) Error code reference: RWPB-VERIFY-###
The installer and preflight tooling surface structured error codes. This doc defines each code, likely causes, and remediation steps.

Collect this for Support (include in ticket)
- Installer version (e.g., redwood-private-installer --version)
- Bundle build ID (from bundle inspect output)
- Full output of: redwood-private-installer bundle inspect <path> --verify
- sha256 of the bundle tarball before/after transfer (if applicable)
- Any local verification audit events (if enabled)

Selected error codes (examples)
- RWPB-VERIFY-001: Signature missing
  Cause: Bundle does not include expected detached signature file (e.g., manifest.sig) or signature path mismatches bundle layout.
  Fix: Re-download bundle; ensure bundle was extracted without dropping files; validate expected layout.

- RWPB-VERIFY-004: Signature invalid
  Cause: Manifest/signature mismatch (tampering, corruption, or wrong signature for the manifest).
  Fix: Treat as potential security incident until proven otherwise. Stop install. Preserve original media. Obtain a fresh bundle from official channel. Re-run bundle inspect --verify. Escalate to Support/Security if repeated.

- RWPB-VERIFY-012: Manifest hash mismatch
  Cause: Artifact referenced in manifest does not match SHA256 in manifest (common after offline transfer/extraction).
  Fix: Compare sha256 before vs after transfer; re-transfer with verified tooling; avoid re-packaging; confirm tar extraction does not normalize line endings or permissions.

- RWPB-VERIFY-021: Unknown signing key
  Cause: Bundle signed by a key not present in local trust store (often after key rotation).
  Fix: Update trust store per key rotation doc; re-verify. Do not bypass as first step.

- RWPB-VERIFY-031: Not-yet-valid / time skew
  Cause: Local system time is behind signature validity start time (common in air-gapped environments without NTP).
  Fix: Correct system time using approved internal process. For diagnostics only, use the documented time override flag to validate whether skew is the root cause.

- RWPB-VERIFY-041: Downgrade prevented
  Cause: Attempting to install a bundle with a signed version lower than what is recorded in local install state.
  Fix: Use supported rollback procedure; do not delete state unless explicitly directed by Support with change control.

Break-glass (last resort)
This PR documents the existence of a break-glass verification override flag for supported installer versions. The docs emphasize: (1) it may be disabled by policy, (2) it emits explicit warnings/audit markers, and (3) it should only be used with Redwood approval for time-bounded emergency remediation.

Why now
- Pilot feedback and SUP tickets show recurring failure modes (hash mismatch after transfer; unknown key post-rotation; time skew causing not-yet-valid).
- Support needed a single page to link in responses instead of ad-hoc guidance.

Links
- Related install guide PR: redwood-docs#1208
- Linear: ENG-4850 (docs/runbooks), ENG-4823 (installer verification), ENG-4826 (inspect CLI)
- Support tickets referenced: SUP-31844, SUP-31892, SUP-31910, SUP-31977

Notes
- Wording intentionally avoids hard commitments for customer-signed bundles; points to roadmap language only.
- Error codes are documented as stable identifiers; exact text may vary slightly across installer versions.

  Cause: Bundle does not include expected detached signature file (e.g., manifest.sig) or signature path mismatches bundle layout.
  Fix: Re-download bundle; ensure bundle was extracted without dropping files; validate expected layout.

- RWPB-VERIFY-004: Signature invalid
  Cause: Manifest/signature mismatch (tampering, corruption, or wrong signature for the manifest).
  Fix: Treat as potential security incident until proven otherwise. Stop install. Preserve original media. Obtain a fresh bundle from official channel. Re-run bundle inspect --verify. Escalate to Support/Security if repeated.

- RWPB-VERIFY-012: Manifest hash mismatch
  Cause: Artifact referenced in manifest does not match SHA256 in manifest (common after offline transfer/extraction).
  Fix: Compare sha256 before vs after transfer; re-transfer with verified tooling; avoid re-packaging; confirm tar extraction does not normalize line endings or permissions.

- RWPB-VERIFY-021: Unknown signing key
  Cause: Bundle signed by a key not present in local trust store (often after key rotation).
  Fix: Update trust store per key rotation doc; re-verify. Do not bypass as first step.

- RWPB-VERIFY-031: Not-yet-valid / time skew
  Cause: Local system time is behind signature validity start time (common in air-gapped environments without NTP).
  Fix: Correct system time using approved internal process. For diagnostics only, use the documented time override flag to validate whether skew is the root cause.

- RWPB-VERIFY-041: Downgrade prevented
  Cause: Attempting to install a bundle with a signed version lower than what is recorded in local install state.
  Fix: Use supported rollback procedure; do not delete state unless explicitly directed by Support with change control.

Break-glass (last resort)
This PR documents the existence of a break-glass verification override flag for supported installer versions. The docs emphasize: (1) it may be disabled by policy, (2) it emits explicit warnings/audit markers, and (3) it should only be used with Redwood approval for time-bounded emergency remediation.

Why now
- Pilot feedback and SUP tickets show recurring failure modes (hash mismatch after transfer; unknown key post-rotation; time skew causing not-yet-valid).
- Support needed a single page to link in responses instead of ad-hoc guidance.

Links
- Related install guide PR: redwood-docs#1208
- Linear: ENG-4850 (docs/runbooks), ENG-4823 (installer verification), ENG-4826 (inspect CLI)
- Support tickets referenced: SUP-31844, SUP-31892, SUP-31910, SUP-31977

Notes
- Wording intentionally avoids hard commitments for customer-signed bundles; points to roadmap language only.
- Error codes are documented as stable identifiers; exact text may vary slightly across installer versions.

title: Private offline bundles: key rotation + trust store update guide (air-gapped)
preview: Summary: Adds customer-facing documentation for signing key rotation and trust store updates for Redwood Private offline bundles in air-gapped environments. This explains (1) how rotations are announced and staged (overlap window with multiple trusted keys), (2) how customers update trust roots offline (via a trust store update package or manual key file update, depending on deployment), and (3) what Support expects when troubleshooting unknown-key and post-rotation verification failures. Motivation: Pilot customers asked for a single canonical reference that answers “what changes on rotation?”, “what do I need to copy into the air-gap?”, and “how do I prove my installer trusts the new key before attempting an upgrade?”. This PR aligns language with installer behavior (fail-closed on unknown key; break-glass requires explicit policy gating and produces audit markers) and with Release Eng’s rotation cadence guidance. Scope: Docs only (no product changes). References: Linear ENG-4825 (trust store / rotation), ENG-4850 (docs + runbooks), PM-894 (GA criteria). Also addresses recurring Support case patterns (SUP-31910: unknown signing key after rotation). Content highlights: - Defines terms: signing key, trust store, key set ID, overlap window, “active signing key” vs “trusted verification key set”. - Rotation lifecycle: announce -> publish trust store update -> overlap window -> switch signing key -> retire old key after minimum window. - Air-gapped update flow: verify checksum/signature of the trust update package out-of-band, transfer via approved media, apply update to installer trust store, verify with `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect --verify` before running upgrade. - Failure handling: clear guidance for error `RWPB-VERIFY-UNK-KEY` (unknown key) and `RWPB-VERIFY-NOTYETVALID` (time skew); includes what logs and commands to collect. - Support expectations: attach `bundle inspect` output, trust store version, installer version, and support bundle (includes verification metadata) where permitted. Review notes: Includes a small FAQ and a one-page runbook excerpt for SE/Support (kept within the Private docs section).

title: Private: Air-gapped installation guide for signed offline bundles
preview:
Motivation: Air-gapped/private customers need a single, end-to-end guide that explains (1) what a signed offline bundle is, (2) how to verify provenance/integrity offline, (3) how installation differs from connected installs, and (4) what to do when verification fails (incl. time skew and key rotation). This PR adds a full guide targeted at Redwood Private operators, plus a concise troubleshooting section and references for Support/SE.

Scope: Documentation only (no product behavior changes). The guide aligns with the installer changes landing in redwood-private-installer (inspect + preflight verify + trust store + break-glass) and the release pipeline guidance from Release Eng.

What’s included:
- New doc: "Air-gapped installation (signed offline bundles)" with prerequisites, bundle transport, verification, install flow, and upgrade notes.
- Step-by-step commands using the installer CLI (inspect/verify) and preflight.
- Troubleshooting section with common error cases: hash mismatch after transfer, unknown signing key after rotation, not-yet-valid due to time skew, and downgrade prevention.
- Operator guidance: what gets logged locally (verification audit events), where to find logs, and how to generate a support bundle.
- Key rotation section: how to update the trust store in an air-gapped env (what file to place, how to validate it), and how to confirm the active signing key.
- Explicit break-glass callout: policy-gated override is documented as last resort with required audit annotations and follow-up actions.

Notable doc decisions:
- Uses a "fail closed" stance in examples: installs should stop on signature/manifest mismatch.
- Places verification before any cluster mutations; includes a suggested two-person check for regulated customers.
- Avoids implying customer-signed bundles are supported today; includes a short "Roadmap" note referencing future work.

Screenshots: none (customers asked for copy/paste CLI first). Added example outputs as fenced blocks.

Requested review focus:
- Security: language around trust roots, key rotation, and break-glass policy wording.
- Release Eng: version pinning and "required installer version" callouts.
- Support/SE: troubleshooting steps map to what we can realistically ask customers to do in air-gapped environments.

title: Document incident taxonomy fields in Ops guide
preview:
Summary
Adds an Ops Guide section documenting the required incident taxonomy fields (incident_type / incident_subtype / resolved_owner), how to choose a classification during a live incident, and how Customer Success / Support should interpret taxonomy-driven dashboards.

Context
As part of the Reliability Program (“Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”), we’re making incident_type a required field in incident-bot and piping it through dashboards/alerts. This PR documents the conventions so on-call classification is consistent and reporting is usable.

What’s included
- New Ops Guide page: “Incident taxonomy fields (required)”.
- A short decision flow for classification (optimize for on-call speed + low ambiguity).
- Guidance on multi-cause incidents: primary incident_type vs contributing systems.
- How the owner is resolved (incident-bot → control plane mapping), and what to do when it’s wrong.
- Dashboard interpretation for CS/Support: what “Unknown/Unclassified” means, how to avoid misreading volume charts, and how to communicate incident type externally.

Key definitions documented
- incident_type: Primary category for reliability reporting + ownership.
- incident_subtype: Narrower bucket used for routing/runbooks where needed.
- resolved_owner: Primary owning team for mitigation + follow-ups (auto-resolved; can be overridden with justification).
- contributing_systems: Freeform list (avoid using this as a substitute for incident_type).

Classification decision flow (high-level)
1) Is this primarily an auth/permissions failure? → Auth/RBAC.
2) Are customers seeing elevated 5xx / timeouts / overload signals? → Availability/Overload.
3) Are p95/p99 latencies elevated without a corresponding 5xx spike? → Latency regression.
4) Are streams resetting / disconnecting / partial responses? → Streaming disconnects.
5) Is traffic being rerouted unexpectedly or fallback behavior is incorrect? → Routing/Fallback misbehavior.
6) Is the service healthy but constrained by fleet/GPU capacity or provisioning lag? → Capacity/Fleet shortage.
7) Are control plane operations failing (policy updates, config writes, rollout controls)? → Control plane outage/degradation.
8) Are we blind (missing metrics/traces/logs) such that we can’t validate impact? → Telemetry blind spot.
9) Private-specific: connectivity/config break (DNS/TLS/VPC routing) → Private deployment connectivity/config.

If multiple apply: pick the type that best describes the customer-visible symptom driving the incident declaration; record other factors under contributing_systems and in the timeline. If truly unclear during mitigation, set incident_type=Unknown and update within 60 minutes (or before resolve), per incident process policy.

CS/Support guidance (dashboards + comms)
- incident_type is intended for operational grouping, not a customer-facing root cause statement. When communicating externally, prefer: “We’re investigating an elevated error rate (Availability) impacting requests in <region>” vs overly specific internal causes.
- “Unknown/Unclassified” should trend down over time; it’s a process metric. A spike often indicates training gaps or tooling regression (e.g., incident-bot modal missing field).
- Compare incident volume by type only within the same tier/time window; Dedicated and Private have different baselines and escalation mechanics.
- Owner in dashboards is for internal accountability; CS should not promise timelines based solely on owner assignment. Use published remediation SLAs and the IC/SRE updates.

Links
- Confluence: Incident taxonomy v1 definitions (SRE)
- Confluence: Ownership mapping + remediation SLA matrix
- ADR-0142: Incident taxonomy as required incident field
- Dashboard spec: Incident taxonomy dashboards (Observability)

Rollout note
This documentation aligns with the incident-bot enforcement change (ENG-4822) and the dashboard rollout (ENG-4828). Please keep this page in sync with taxonomy v1.1 changes; do not add new incident_type values in docs without SRE sign-off.

Checklist
- [x] Terminology consistent with taxonomy v1
- [x] Includes examples for common confusion cases (capacity vs routing, streaming vs client)
- [x] CS/Support read-only interpretation guidance reviewed
- [x] Mentions cardinality constraints (avoid inventing new tags)

Screenshots
Included in the doc: incident-bot modal (required fields) + dashboard filter examples (incident_type, owner, tier).

title: Document 429 reason codes, headers, and retry guidance for hot-route capacity protection
preview:
## Summary
This PR updates Redwood docs to reflect the new tier-aware rate limiting / quota enforcement and overload protection behavior being rolled out for hot enterprise routes. It documents:
- The standardized 429 error payload shape used across Hosted API endpoints
- The set of 429 reason codes and how to interpret them (quota vs rate limit vs overload shedding)
- Response headers clients should read (including Retry-After) and recommended retry/backoff strategies
- How enterprise priority works under fleet pressure (what is and isn’t guaranteed)

This is intended to reduce customer confusion during rollout and give Support consistent language to reference.

## Why
We’ve historically overloaded multiple concepts into “429 Too Many Requests” with inconsistent messages across routes and SDKs. With hot-route capacity protection enabled, customers will see more predictable throttling for best-effort traffic, and enterprise traffic will be protected by priority admission control. We need clear docs so developers can:
- Distinguish between quota exhaustion and overload shedding
- Implement correct backoff and avoid synchronized retries
- Understand what “enterprise priority” means (and what it does not mean)

Related incidents/tickets prompting clarity:
- SUP-18421 (enterprise reported a 429 spike after priority routing rollout; unclear which limits applied)
- SUP-18462 (streaming clients saw inconsistent termination semantics on shed)

## What changed
### 1) Standardized 429 payload + reason codes
Added a dedicated section to `docs/api-reference/errors.md` and a new guide at `docs/hosted-api/handling-429s.md`.

Documented reason codes (current set):
- `quota_exceeded` — Account/org/project quota depleted for the current window.
- `rate_limited` — Request rate exceeded configured limit (short window protection).
- `overload_shed` — Request rejected due to fleet pressure / admission control to protect SLOs.
- `tier_capacity_protection` — Request rejected because best-effort capacity was constrained and enterprise budgets were reserved.
- `route_budget_exhausted` — Protected route budget depleted for the tier in the current control interval.

Notes included in docs:
- Not all codes apply to all endpoints.
- Codes are stable identifiers; human-readable messages may change.
- Customers should branch on `code` and/or headers, not string-match messages.

### 2) Response headers + semantics
Documented headers returned on 429 responses:
- `Retry-After`: integer seconds when a bounded retry window is known (best effort; may be omitted)
- `x-redwood-request-id`: request correlation ID for Support/SRE
- `x-redwood-limit-scope`: `org` | `project` | `api_key` (where applicable)
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-policy`: policy identifier (low cardinality; useful for escalation)
- `x-redwood-overload-mode`: `none` | `admission_control` | `shedding` (only present for overload-related 429s)

Docs explicitly call out that customers may receive 429 without `Retry-After` during fast-changing overload, and should apply client-side exponential backoff with jitter.

### 3) Retry/backoff guidance (streaming + non-streaming)
Added guidance with concrete examples:
- Non-streaming: exponential backoff w/ decorrelated jitter, honor `Retry-After` when present.
- Streaming: treat an early-terminated stream with a final 429-equivalent error as retryable only when the reason code indicates rate limiting / overload (not quota exhaustion). The docs include a warning about duplicate side effects for tool-calling workflows.

### 4) Enterprise priority explanation
New page: `docs/hosted-api/enterprise-priority-and-capacity-protection.md`.
It explains:
- Enterprise traffic is assigned a higher priority class on protected routes and should remain within published SLOs during moderate overload conditions.
- Best-effort traffic is throttled earlier and more predictably to preserve fleet health.
- Priority does not mean unlimited capacity; severe regional incidents can still cause 429/5xx.
- How to request temporary exceptions (burst allowances) and what information Support will ask for.

## Examples included in docs
### Example 429 response (quota)
```http
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
Retry-After: 60
x-redwood-request-id: req_01JFK9Q2QY7X1N8B1M6P4A6R9Z
x-redwood-limit-scope: project
x-redwood-rate-limit-policy: hosted-default-quota-v2

{
  "error": {
    "type": "rate_limit_error",
    "code": "quota_exceeded",
    "message": "Quota exceeded for this project. Please retry after the window resets or request a quota increase."
  }
}
```

### Example 429 response (overload shedding)
```http
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
x-redwood-request-id: req_01JFK9T1S8Z7QWQ5C3QXH4D9AD
x-redwood-overload-mode: shedding
x-redwood-rate-limit-policy: hot-route-protection-v1

{
  "error": {
    "type": "rate_limit_error",
    "code": "overload_shed",
    "message": "Request rejected due to temporary capacity pressure. Please retry with backoff."
  }
}
```

## Validation
- Ran docs build locally (`pnpm docs:build`) and link-check.
- Added a small script guard (`scripts/validate-error-codes.ts`) to ensure the reason code list stays in sync with the canonical list in docs (reduces drift during rollout).

## Rollout / timing notes
This PR is safe to merge before GA; it uses wording aligned with the current canary behavior and avoids promising specific numeric limits. The changelog entry frames this as “clarified 429 semantics and new reason codes/headers” without implying a billing change.

## Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Add SDK-specific retry snippets (Python/TS/Go) once SDK teams land their corresponding helpers.
- Add a Console screenshot once internal UI hooks for viewing tier limits are stable.

Co-authored-by: Dev Patel (Support copy edits)
Reviewed-by: Elliot Price (Docs), Amara Diallo (Gateway semantics), Rafael Mendes (SRE), Nikhil Sharma (Product)

title: Docs: clarify public status page components + how to read incident updates
preview:
## Summary
This PR updates customer-facing docs so the public status page is easier to interpret (what each component represents) and incident updates are more consistent (what each update state means, what info is included/excluded).

This work supports the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative by reducing ambiguity around component naming and setting correct expectations during active incidents.

## Why
We’ve had repeated customer feedback that the status page was “too coarse” (e.g., Embeddings failures while API looked healthy, streaming-only degradations not reflected clearly). Support also asked for a canonical reference they can link in tickets when customers dispute accuracy.

Key goals for the copy:
- Explain component boundaries in plain language (without leaking internal service topology)
- Define incident update phases + typical contents
- Avoid over-promising SLAs or update cadence
- Clarify how Dedicated/Private incidents are handled on the public page

## What changed
- Added a new doc page: **“Status page components”** with:
  - Component definitions (Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings vs Reranking vs Console)
  - Region scoping guidance (e.g., US-East vs EU-West components)
  - Common examples of “what is / isn’t included”
- Updated incident comms guidance:
  - What each status value means (Operational / Degraded Performance / Partial Outage / Major Outage)
  - What each incident update state means (Investigating / Identified / Mitigating / Monitoring / Resolved)
  - Explicit note: timelines and scope may change during investigation; we’ll correct component selection if new evidence emerges
- Added FAQ targeted at common customer questions:
  - “Why does my app fail but status shows Operational?”
  - “Do you post latency-only issues?”
  - “How do I subscribe to updates / where are post-incident summaries?”
- Dedicated/Private notes:
  - Dedicated: public page may reflect shared control-plane signals and region-level issues; workload/customer-specific degradations may not be publicly visible
  - Private: customer-managed environments are not represented on the public status page; customers should follow their private support/escalation channel

## Content details (copy excerpts)
**New component definition table (excerpt):**
- *Hosted API – Chat/Completions (US-East)*: Request routing + synchronous generation endpoints in US-East. Does not include Streaming disconnects if non-streaming traffic is unaffected.
- *Hosted API – Streaming (US-East)*: SSE/websocket streaming connection stability and stream delivery. Does not imply non-streaming endpoints are impacted.
- *Hosted API – Embeddings (Global)*: Embeddings endpoints across regions; incidents may be marked per-region when impact is isolated.
- *Console*: Console access, incident/summaries viewing, and operational dashboards; does not affect inference availability directly.

**Incident update state guidance (excerpt):**
- *Investigating*: We’re aware of a potential issue and are validating scope/impact. Components may be adjusted as we confirm evidence.
- *Identified*: Root cause (or likely cause) is known and we’re working on mitigation.
- *Mitigating*: Active mitigation underway (rollback, capacity shift, failover, etc.).
- *Monitoring*: Mitigation is in place; we’re monitoring to confirm recovery and prevent regression.
- *Resolved*: Systems are stable; post-incident summary will be published when available.

## Files changed
- `docs/status-page/components.md` (new)
- `docs/status-page/incident-updates.md` (expanded: definitions + examples)
- `docs/status-page/index.md` (navigation + cross-links)
- `docs/support/incident-escalations.md` (added “link customers here” guidance)
- `docs/glossary.md` (added/normalized terms: Partial Outage, Degraded Performance)
- `docs/_includes/statuspage-component-callout.md` (new callout block reused across pages)
- `docs/assets/statuspage-component-example.png` (small annotated screenshot)
- `docs/README.md` (contribution note: don’t add customer-specific examples)
- `docs/redirects.yml` (redirect old “status” URL to new index)

## Testing / validation
- Ran docs build + link check locally
- Verified redirects do not break existing inbound URLs from Support macros
- Proofread against template language constraints (avoid: exact update interval promises; avoid: confirming customer-specific impact)

CI checks:
- `docs-build` ✅
- `markdownlint` ✅
- `link-check` ✅

## Review notes / discussion
- **Wei Chen**: requested softer language around uncertainty early in incidents; replaced “we will confirm” with “we are validating” and added explicit note that affected components may be updated as evidence changes.
- **Dev Patel (Support)**: requested an FAQ entry + a single canonical URL to share in disputed-status tickets; added “If you believe status is inaccurate” section with what info to include when contacting Support.
- **Paula Mendes**: requested alignment with the new incident template verbs (“Mitigating” vs “Mitigation in progress”); updated wording to match the internal template phases.
- **Michael Grant (Legal)**: requested removal of any implied SLA/cadence language; removed “updates every X minutes” phrasing and replaced with “we aim to provide timely updates as we validate scope.”
- **Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance)**: requested explicit statement that Dedicated/Private customer-specific incidents may not be publicly disclosed; added “visibility and confidentiality” note.

## Checklist
- [x] Copy aligns with status page taxonomy (component naming)
- [x] No customer-identifying details / no internal topology disclosure
- [x] No SLA / cadence commitments
- [x] Support-friendly canonical link + FAQ

## Follow-ups
- PM-612: add a public post-incident summary format page (separate PR)
- DES-245: ensure status page UI labels match component display names used in docs

Merged via squash after approvals; commit message: `Docs: clarify status page components + incident update guidance`.
