title: Key rotation procedure + audit evidence checklist (KMS/Private)
preview:
Summary: Adds customer/operator-facing documentation for key rotation in Redwood Private/Dedicated contexts, plus an audit-friendly evidence checklist that maps cleanly to common SOC2/ISO narratives. This PR is intended to reduce back-and-forth during enterprise security reviews by making our rotation steps and evidence artifacts explicit and repeatable.

Context / why: Regulated customers (and prospects) are asking for (a) a clear rotation procedure (including rollback criteria) and (b) the exact evidence they can expect to produce/receive (who approved, what changed, when it happened, what logs prove it). This doc set aligns with the ongoing KMS hardening effort and the rotation strategy in ADR-008.

What’s included: 1) New page: “Key rotation procedure (KMS envelope encryption)” covering prerequisites, roles/responsibilities (Customer vs Redwood), staged rollout, dual-read window, cutover, verification, and rollback. 2) New page: “Rotation evidence checklist” that can be used as a runbook attachment and as audit evidence. Includes templates for a rotation log entry, required screenshots/exports, and pointers to audit log event types. 3) Updates to navigation + cross-links from the Private KMS Operator Guide and Security FAQ sections to reduce discoverability issues.

Audience: Private operators, SRE/on-call, Security/GRC reviewers, Sales/SE support during enterprise security reviews.

Notes on scope: This is documentation-only and does not introduce new technical behavior. It references implementation behavior that is landing in the core repo (dual-read support, audit log events, healthchecks). Where behavior differs between Hosted vs Private, we call it out explicitly.

References: - ADR-008 (Key rotation strategy: dual-read window + cutover criteria) - Linear: ENG-4826 (evidence artifacts), PM-913 (customer expectations), ENG-4821 (initiative epic) - Support: SUP-19405 (customer evidence request / cutover questions)

Content highlights (key decisions captured in docs): - Rotation cadence: documented as customer-driven (typical quarterly/annual), with Redwood providing a repeatable procedure + evidence artifacts; the doc avoids committing to a universal cadence but includes guidance and defaults used in regulated deployments. - Dual-read / dual-decrypt window: documented as the recommended approach to avoid hard cutovers; includes a minimum observation window and explicit cutover criteria (error-rate and decrypt-mismatch signals). - Rollback: explicitly documented; includes triggers (e.g., elevated KMS errors, decrypt mismatches) and the fastest safe rollback path (toggle to prior key version + keep dual-read during investigation). - Evidence: checklist enumerates approvals, change tickets, timestamps, config diffs, audit log exports, and validation outputs from installer preflight (Private).

Screenshots/examples: Redacted examples are included (ARNs, tenant IDs, regions). Please flag anything that looks too close to production formatting.

Test plan: - Local docs build: `pnpm install && pnpm build` - Link check: `pnpm lint:links` - Preview reviewed in Netlify: see CI checks.

Checklist: - [x] Reviewed with Security (terminology + evidence expectations) - [x] Reviewed with SRE (rollback triggers + operational steps) - [x] Reviewed with Product (customer-facing positioning) - [x] Added cross-links from Private KMS operator guide - [x] Added “last reviewed” footer + owners for ongoing maintenance

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Once PR redwood/pr-18541 lands, update evidence page to include final audit log event names/fields (placeholders included). - Once dashboards ship (observability-pack/pr-844), add a “Verification dashboards” subsection with canonical panel links per environment.

title: Private: publish KMS integration operator guide (setup, validation, troubleshooting)
preview:
Context
Regulated customers evaluating Redwood Private are consistently asking for a single, operator-oriented KMS integration guide that explains (1) what Redwood encrypts and where envelope encryption happens, (2) which permissions are required and why, (3) how to validate the integration before turning on production traffic, and (4) what the expected system behavior is during KMS degradation/outage.

This PR publishes the first version of the "Private deployment: Customer KMS integration operator guide" on the docs site, incorporating feedback from Support (SUP-19341 / SUP-19377) and Sales/SE security reviews (SUP-19405). It is written to be evidence-friendly for enterprise security questionnaires and to reduce back-and-forth during onboarding.

What’s included
- New operator guide page for Private deployments covering: prerequisites, supported KMS patterns (AWS/GCP/Azure conceptual flows), required IAM permissions, key/region configuration requirements, and key ID/ARN gotchas.
- A validation/preflight section that mirrors the installer checks (linking out to the installer’s validation report format) and provides copy/paste commands/operators steps to confirm access.
- Troubleshooting matrix with symptoms -> likely root cause -> how to confirm -> remediation, focused on the failure modes we see most often: AccessDenied, throttling, timeouts/network issues, wrong key ID/region, key disabled, and ciphertext mismatch during rotation.
- “Operational behavior” section describing Redwood’s safe defaults: timeouts/retry budget guidance, circuit-breaker/degrade behavior at a high level, and when operators should page Redwood vs investigate their KMS/IAM.
- Cross-links to the rotation procedure page (to be published in a follow-up PR) and internal runbooks for SRE (for Redwood staff only).

What’s not included (intentionally)
- No new cryptographic primitives/algorithms; this is operational documentation only.
- No provider-specific Terraform modules or customer IAM templates embedded inline; we instead link to the Private deployment reference config examples and keep this guide focused on the “what/why/how to validate.”

Information architecture
This guide lives under: Private Deployments -> Security & Compliance -> Key management (KMS).

Screenshots/diagrams
- Included one simple sequence diagram for envelope encryption flow in Private.
- Avoided UI screenshots that will churn; instead used CLI and configuration snippets.

Verification
- Local build: `pnpm i && pnpm build`.
- Link check: `pnpm lint:links`.
- Verified no broken anchors in the new troubleshooting matrix.

Follow-ups
- Rotation procedure + evidence checklist PR (tracked in PM-913 / ENG-4826).
- Add a short "KMS readiness" checklist to the Private install/upgrade guide landing page.

Checklist
- [x] Docs align with Security review checklist terminology (AccessDenied vs Unauthorized; "key disabled" vs "pending deletion")
- [x] Troubleshooting content validated against recent Support tickets (SUP-19341, SUP-19377)
- [x] Added explicit guidance on least-privilege vs break-glass roles (operator action vs Redwood action)
- [x] Included “known pitfalls” callouts: wrong region, alias vs key ID confusion, missing `kms:Decrypt` on runtime role

Notes for reviewers
- Please scrutinize the troubleshooting table for accuracy and whether the remediation steps are actionable.
- SRE: please confirm the “expected behavior during KMS throttling/outage” section matches current alerting and on-call guidance.
- Security: please confirm we are not overpromising (especially around audit logs and rotation).

title: Internal ops docs: Provision-to-ready readiness states, remediation, and dashboards/runbooks
preview:
Motivation: With the provision-to-ready automation rollout, we need a single, up-to-date internal operator guide that explains (a) what each readiness state means, (b) what to do when nodes are stuck or failing validations, and (c) where to look first (dashboards/logs/runbooks) without rediscovering tribal knowledge in Slack threads. This PR updates the internal docs to match ADR-017 readiness semantics and the current control-plane/Fleet Readiness Service behavior, and standardizes remediation guidance (drain/quarantine/reimage order) to reduce risk during on-call.

Summary of changes: Adds an operator-focused "Provision-to-ready operations" page that describes the canonical states (Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready/Quarantined), the authoritative signals for transitions (agent heartbeat + validation artifacts + control-plane writes), and practical triage flows for common failures (kubelet join, driver parity, GPU health, NCCL smoke test, perf canary). Adds a "stuck in Validating" decision tree with time-based thresholds and clear ownership boundaries (Infra/Fleet vs Platform vs SRE). Links the docs to the current dashboards and runbooks, and includes a quickstart section for internal console usage (filters, drill-down, and where to find failure reasons/artifacts). Also documents the image channel rollout + rollback basics (candidate/stable), including the audit event trail for channel changes.

Notable clarifications: (1) Quarantine is a safety state used when failures repeat or indicate hardware/network fabric issues; it is not a generic catch-all for "slow provisioning". (2) Reimage is the preferred first-line remediation for image/driver drift failures; quarantine is preferred for GPU health or NCCL/fabric failures after retry policy is exhausted. (3) Do not manually set readiness state except via the approved operator workflow; state changes are audited.

Docs links added/updated in this PR: Provision-to-ready failures triage runbook; SLO/time-to-ready page; dashboard catalog; gameday plan; ADR-017 reference; Fleet Readiness Service API overview.

Rollout note: These docs assume the staged rollout feature flag is enabled only for us-east A100 pools; wording is careful to avoid implying global availability. References SUP-28433 for the "stuck in Validating" support pattern and INT-1042 for dashboard access/least-privilege.

Screenshots: Included updated internal console screenshots (redacted pool IDs) showing readiness state filters and failure reason drill-down.

Testing: Ran docs build locally and link checker; CI includes markdown lint + internal link validation.

Links the docs to the current dashboards and runbooks, and includes a quickstart section for internal console usage (filters, drill-down, and where to find failure reasons/artifacts). Also documents the image channel rollout + rollback basics (candidate/stable), including the audit event trail for channel changes.

Notable clarifications: (1) Quarantine is a safety state used when failures repeat or indicate hardware/network fabric issues; it is not a generic catch-all for "slow provisioning". (2) Reimage is the preferred first-line remediation for image/driver drift failures; quarantine is preferred for GPU health or NCCL/fabric failures after retry policy is exhausted. (3) Do not manually set readiness state except via the approved operator workflow; state changes are audited.

Docs links added/updated in this PR: Provision-to-ready failures triage runbook; SLO/time-to-ready page; dashboard catalog; gameday plan; ADR-017 reference; Fleet Readiness Service API overview.

Rollout note: These docs assume the staged rollout feature flag is enabled only for us-east A100 pools; wording is careful to avoid implying global availability. References SUP-28433 for the "stuck in Validating" support pattern and INT-1042 for dashboard access/least-privilege.

Screenshots: Included updated internal console screenshots (redacted pool IDs) showing readiness state filters and failure reason drill-down.

Testing: Ran docs build locally and link checker; CI includes markdown lint + internal link validation.

title: Dedicated: document autoscaling bounds + predictive mode (config, semantics, troubleshooting)
preview: Summary This PR updates customer-facing documentation for Dedicated autoscaling policy bounds and Predictive Mode. It is written to support pilot->GA rollout and reduce support load by clarifying semantics (what is enforced, what is advisory), recommended starting ranges, and what to do when customers hit max bounds or experience queueing during ramps. The new docs cover: configuring min/max replicas (and GPU equivalent guidance), cooldown behavior, Predictive Mode basics, signal arbitration (reactive overrides predictions), and troubleshooting steps with references to the new dashboard panels/metrics. Motivation Recent Dedicated escalations (SUP-28411/SUP-28437) and pilot feedback indicated confusion around (1) whether max is a hard ceiling, (2) why scale-up may stop while queue grows, (3) how Predictive Mode interacts with reactive signals and cooldowns, and (4) what customers should do during region capacity shortages where min cannot be met. This PR consolidates answers into a single doc set and aligns wording with the ADR semantics (hard invariants: never exceed max, never drop below min; deterministic clamping; emit bounded-state signals). Scope / What changed - Added docs page: "Dedicated autoscaling policy" (min/max, cooldowns, predictive toggle, examples for common workloads) - Added section: "Predictive Mode" (what it is, required history, confidence, staleness, fallback to reactive) - Added "Recommended starting points" including baseline min for SLA protection and max as spend guardrail; includes guidance to iterate based on observed queue depth and bound-hit rate - Added troubleshooting playbook: "Max-bound saturation", "Oscillation/jitter", "Forecast stale/unavailable", "Min not met due to capacity shortage" with suggested mitigations and when to contact support - Updated API/Console configuration snippets and terminology to match implementation (field names and UI labels) - Added small FAQ (e.g., "Does Predictive Mode scale to zero?", "Can I exceed max during incidents?" -> no; use contract/CS process) Not in scope - No product behavior changes; this is documentation only - No full runbook content (SRE runbook lives in internal Confluence); we link to customer-facing troubleshooting steps and support contact points Testing - Docs site build + link check - Verified code snippets against Console API endpoint shapes referenced in ENG-22141 (as of 2025-08-21) Rollout notes - Docs are written to be feature-flag aware: clearly marked "Available by request during pilot" and "GA" sections. Where behavior differs between pilot builds and GA target (e.g., confidence indicator display), language is guarded to avoid overpromising. Screenshots are intentionally minimal; we rely on text that matches UX labels in DES-981 to avoid churn during final UI polish. Links - Linear: PM-1842, ENG-22149 - Support references: SUP-28501 (how to set bounds), SUP-28437 (daily ramp), SUP-28479 (oscillation) - Internal semantics: ADR "Autoscaling bounds precedence and safety invariants" (referenced but not linked in public docs) - Observability: references the public-facing metric names that appear in the Dedicated dashboard (bound hit rate, desired vs bounded replicas, queue depth) Changed areas/files (high-level) - docs/dedicated/autoscaling-policy.md (new) - docs/dedicated/predictive-mode.md (new) - docs/dedicated/troubleshooting/autoscaling.md (new) - docs/reference/api/dedicated-scaling-policy.md (updated) - docs/console/dedicated-scaling-policy-ui.md (updated) - docs/shared/glossary.md (updated terms: bound, desired, bounded desired, cooldown) - redirects + nav sidebar updates Example snippet added (from docs) JSON example: { "autoscaling_policy": { "min_replicas": 4, "max_replicas": 24, "cooldown_seconds": 180, "predictive_mode": "ENABLED" } } Notes: - The system will clamp requested desired replicas into [min_replicas, max_replicas]. - If predictive signal is stale/unavailable, the autoscaler falls back to reactive scaling (still respecting bounds).

title: Docs: Private observability minimum pack (install, dashboards, alerts, log export examples)
preview:
Context: This PR documents the v1 “Private observability minimum pack” shipping with Redwood Private (VPC and on-prem). The goal is to make installs supportable without bespoke enablement by providing (1) clear install/upgrade steps, (2) a stable dashboard catalog, (3) actionable alert descriptions with runbook links, and (4) supported log export examples suitable for private environments.

What’s included: Adds a new doc set under /private/observability/minimum-pack covering prerequisites (Prometheus/Grafana/Loki optional), Helm values required for the observability-pack integration, and post-install validation steps that match installer checks. Documents the dashboard catalog (Runtime Overview, Control Plane Health, GPU/Node Saturation, Queueing/Batching, and Prometheus Health) with “what to look at” notes and ownership. Adds an alert reference section mapping each baseline alert to severity, likely causes, and first actions (including how to silence/tune safely). Adds log pipeline/export guidance: recommended structured log fields, redaction rules, retention defaults, and tested sink examples for Loki and Splunk HEC (plus “bring-your-own” notes for ELK/OTLP).

Not included / non-goals: No premium/federated observability features (multi-cluster aggregation, long retention sizing, full tracing sampling policy UI). This doc intentionally focuses on what Support/SRE will treat as “supported minimum.”

Why now: Support cases SUP-49210 and SUP-49238 showed gaps in “what should I see after install/upgrade?” and “which alerts are expected/noisy?”. This doc set provides a single canonical reference and standardizes runbook links embedded in alert annotations.

Notes: The configuration examples are written to be air-gap friendly (no external chart repos required at runtime) and avoid advising customers to increase metric label cardinality. Security guidance explicitly calls out that request/response payload logging is disabled by default and should remain off for most customers.

Validation: Verified docs against current Helm values from redwood-helm-charts wiring and smoke test harness outputs; cross-checked alert names and dashboard UIDs with observability-pack bundle version v0.9.3.

Checklist: [x] Install steps include both Helm-only and installer-managed flows [x] Includes post-install verification and common failure modes (scrapes, datasource auth) [x] Runbook links included in each alert meaning section [x] Security review notes for redaction/retention [x] Support enablement section updated (what info to request in an escalation)

Example snippet (Splunk HEC values): helm upgrade --install redwood-private redwood/private -f values.yaml --set observability.logs.enabled=true --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.enabled=true --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.endpoint=https://splunk.example:8088/services/collector --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.tokenFromSecret.name=splunk-hec --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.tokenFromSecret.key=token

Example snippet (Loki values): observability: { logs: { enabled: true, sinks: { loki: { enabled: true, endpoint: "http://loki.monitoring.svc:3100" } } } }

title: Update Private upgrade docs: new installer commands, troubleshooting, rollback guidance
preview: Summary: Align Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) upgrade/rollback documentation with the new installer workflow: plan -> prechecks -> backup -> apply -> verify -> rollback (if needed). This updates the operator-facing steps, introduces troubleshooting playbooks for common precheck and upgrade failures, and clarifies rollback guarantees/limitations (esp. around schema migrations and partial rollouts). Motivation: Support escalations and design partner feedback indicated the previous docs were ambiguous about (a) which checks are blocking vs warning, (b) what gets backed up and how to validate restore readiness, and (c) when rollback is safe vs when manual intervention is required. This PR is the docs companion to private-installer changes (upgrade plan generator, preflight framework, backup orchestrator, rollback state machine) and Helm ordering changes (control plane before data plane). Audience/visibility: Internal runbooks + external-ish docs pages that are shared with Private customers under guidance. What changed (high level): 1) Rewrote the main upgrade procedure to use new commands and phase naming: Generate plan, Run prechecks, Create backup, Apply upgrade (control plane then data plane), Post-upgrade verification, Capture artifacts/support bundle. 2) Added a troubleshooting index for: precheck failures (storage headroom, DB connectivity, OIDC reachability, TLS/DNS), migration lock timeouts, stuck Helm hooks/jobs, artifact/bundle verification errors for air-gapped upgrades, and rollback failures. 3) Added explicit rollback guidance: rollback entry points, expected time, health checks, and a decision tree for 'rollback vs fix-forward' based on stage. 4) Updated on-prem/air-gapped section with offline bundle verification expectations (checksums/signatures), mirror endpoints, and common operator mistakes. 5) Added an 'Upgrade artifacts for auditability' section describing what the installer persists (plan JSON, precheck results, backup IDs, execution log, audit events) and what to attach to support tickets. Notes: This PR intentionally does not introduce net-new policy; it documents the gating/checklist already being formalized elsewhere. Where behavior is still being finalized, wording is 'as of Private installer >= 1.9.0' and points to release notes. Checklist: - [x] Steps match installer CLI (plan/apply/rollback) - [x] Troubleshooting covers top 5 support cases (SUP-18421, SUP-18488, SUP-18510, SUP-18577) - [x] On-prem/air-gapped guidance includes bundle verification and offline registries - [x] Security reviewed language around backups/secrets (no plaintext exports, KMS/HSM expectations) - [x] Link check + docs build + Vale pass

title: Document Dedicated Burst: behavior, limits, denial reasons, and client retry guidance
preview: Summary: This PR adds customer-facing documentation for the new Dedicated “Burst Capacity” option: what it is (borrowing temporary capacity from Redwood-managed burst pools), how limits/guardrails work, how to configure it (what’s controlled by Redwood vs what customers can tune), and what clients should do when burst is denied. Motivation: During beta we saw repeated support questions around (a) why burst was denied even when baseline looked “underused”, (b) what HTTP status / reason codes mean, and (c) how retries can accidentally amplify load or trigger double-counting concerns. This doc set aims to make behavior predictable and reduce time-to-resolution for incidents. Scope: Documented (1) baseline vs burst semantics, (2) per-tenant caps (max burst ratio, absolute cap), cooldown windows, and fleet safety caps at a high level, (3) denial reasons and what the platform does (fallback vs immediate backpressure), and (4) recommended client retry patterns with Retry-After handling + jitter and idempotency guidance. Non-goals: This does not document internal pool sizing policy, exact threshold numbers (these are contract/config dependent), or any cross-region bursting (not supported in initial GA). Notable behavior notes included in docs: Burst is best-effort and admission-controlled; baseline Dedicated capacity is not reduced by enabling burst. Burst is constrained to the same region and hardware class as the Dedicated baseline. When burst is denied, the platform returns a deterministic denial reason code and (when applicable) a Retry-After value; clients should not spin in tight loops. When the fleet safety circuit breaker is active, burst admissions may be broadly denied to protect non-burst SLOs. Customer guidance highlights: Prefer app-level smoothing (queues) for batch jobs; avoid synchronized retries across workers; use exponential backoff with full jitter; honor Retry-After; set an overall deadline; and ensure request idempotency keys are stable across retries. Checklists: Docs reviewed by Product + SRE; includes Support-facing “how to explain denials” section; includes example curl/SDK snippets; links to dashboards and runbook placeholders for CS enablement. Screenshots: None added in this PR (Console UI landing in a separate change); docs reference the Console location and API fields customers should expect to see. Testing: Docs build + link check in CI; manual preview in staging docs environment.

title: Console docs: Hosted canary rollouts GA (concepts, workflow, API, troubleshooting)
preview: Summary: This PR refreshes and GA-izes our documentation for Hosted canary rollouts in Redwood Console. It consolidates beta-era notes into a single GA narrative, adds a step-by-step UI walkthrough (wizard + rollout details view), documents the relevant API surfaces at a conceptual level (with request/response snippets), and adds troubleshooting guidance aligned with the current orchestrator + policy guardrails + auto-stop behavior. Motivation: Support and design partners repeatedly asked for a single source-of-truth doc that explains (1) what a Hosted canary rollout is, (2) how percentage splits behave, (3) what guardrails/approvals exist, (4) what happens on SLO breach / auto-pause / rollback, and (5) how to debug split mismatches and “stuck” steps. This PR is intended to be the doc companion to the Hosted canary workflow GA effort. What’s included: (1) New docs page: “Hosted canary rollouts (GA)” covering core concepts (baseline vs canary, cohorting, step schedule, soak time, stop conditions), limitations, and FAQs; (2) Console walkthrough: Create rollout → Review & validate → Start → Progress step → Pause/Resume → Rollback, with screenshots callouts and copy consistent with the wizard; (3) Guardrails and approvals: explains max step size / minimum soak time / approval gates at a user-facing level, with examples of policy validation errors; (4) Observability: how to interpret baseline vs canary cohort metrics (latency p50/p95, error rate, timeouts) and how auto-stop decisions are made; (5) Troubleshooting: common issues (split mismatch, unexpected auto-pause, rollout cannot progress due to soak/approval) and what to collect for support (rollout_id, route, region, timestamps, activity log events); (6) Security/governance notes: audit log event types and what fields customers can expect to see; (7) Updated docs navigation + redirects from the beta page. Not included: Dedicated/Private rollout parity docs, automated quality-gate setup, and deep policy language reference (those remain follow-ups / internal docs). Screenshots: redacted tenant names + routes; updated to current Console build as of 2025-03-26. Testing: link checker, docs build, and snippet lint pass. Rollout: will publish with Console GA announcement; support macros will link to the troubleshooting section; release notes draft updated separately.

title: Route experiments: add customer guide + stickiness key and metrics interpretation
preview: This PR adds the initial customer-facing documentation for Route experiments (route-level A/B/n testing) in Redwood Console Rollouts. It focuses on: (1) what route experiments are and when to use them, (2) how deterministic assignment works and how to choose/pass a stickiness key to avoid skew, (3) how to interpret the built-in metrics (latency/cost/error/throughput + optional quality signals) and guardrails in Console, and (4) troubleshooting common beta issues (missing attribution on streaming retries, propagation lag/stale results, skew when sticky key is low entropy). The guide is intentionally light on statistical significance claims; it documents basic deltas and guardrail thresholds only (per GA scope). It also documents headers and SDK helpers surfaced during beta so users can self-debug variant assignment. Includes updates to the navigation, terminology glossary, and a small FAQ entry on audit logs and RBAC. Related: ENG-9032 (docs), PM-482 (GA requirements). Support context: SUP-24877 (streaming attribution), SUP-24910 (stale results), SUP-24944 (audit export fields). Checklist: [x] New guide page + screenshots/placeholders [x] Stickiness key guidance + examples (Python/TS) [x] Metrics definitions (p50/p95/p99, cost attribution, error rate) [x] Troubleshooting + known pitfalls [x] Link to runbooks/Console UI concepts without exposing internal-only endpoints [x] Doc site nav + lint/link checks

title: Incident Bot v2 operator guide + troubleshooting (deploy/config diff/saturation cards)
preview: This PR updates the operator-facing documentation for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (GA-ready). The goal is to make the bot usable in the first 5 minutes of an incident by standardizing what each context card means, how to validate it, and what to do when it’s missing/empty due to permissions or upstream timeouts. The guide also documents degraded-mode behavior (cached context + links) and the security model for tenant-safe config diffs (redaction + audit logging + break-glass). Scope: docs only (no product/runtime changes). Motivation: recent incident retros and the beta rotation feedback showed that responders could see the cards but weren’t sure what thresholds/links were authoritative, and we repeatedly re-taught the same troubleshooting steps in #help. This adds a single canonical “Operator guide” that Support/SRE/On-call can link during active incidents. Contents: (1) Overview of v2 cards (Recent deploys / Config diff / Saturation signals / Error budget burn links). (2) Interpretation + common pitfalls: staged rollouts, partial propagation, canary vs full, tenant pinning, routing policy interactions, and why GPU% alone is not actionable without queue depth and admission/backpressure. (3) Troubleshooting playbook: empty cards, permission denied, redaction warnings, telemetry query timeouts, and how to switch to link-only mode. (4) Deep link standards into Console (service/region/time window/tenant safe params) and how to widen/narrow time ranges. (5) Security notes: what the bot is allowed to show in Slack, how to request elevated access, and what audit events are emitted. (6) Rollout notes: feature-flag cohorts, GA announcement template, and rollback criteria (docs references only; implementation lives elsewhere).

title: Private: publish backup/restore guide + DR checklists + troubleshooting
preview:
This PR publishes customer-consumable documentation for Redwood Private backup/restore and disaster recovery (DR), aligned with the Day-2 operations toolkit shipping in the private installer and Helm charts. The goal is to give enterprise operators a repeatable path to: (1) configure backup storage securely, (2) take consistent control-plane backups, (3) perform restores into a clean or existing cluster, and (4) validate DR readiness via checklists and periodic restore drills.

What’s included (new/updated docs pages):
- Private Backup & Restore Guide (end-to-end): prerequisites, supported storage backends, encryption/key management, backup create/verify flow, retention, and operational recommendations.
- DR checklists by archetype:
  - VPC single-region restore into clean cluster (same region): DNS/TLS considerations, restore ordering, post-restore validation.
  - On-prem / air-gapped DR: offline bundle expectations, checksum/signature verification, and restore flow without outbound dependencies.
- Troubleshooting + FAQ: common failure modes (RBAC, missing CRDs, slow object stores/NFS timeouts, clock skew), what is and isn’t backed up, and support boundaries.

Key clarifications (based on SRE + Security review):
- Secrets: backups must not contain plaintext secret material. Documentation explicitly states we back up references/metadata (and required config state) and rely on customer-managed secret stores/KMS for secret material. Where a secret must exist (e.g., restore job credentials), the docs call out operator action and validation steps.
- Encryption: artifacts are encrypted at rest; envelope encryption is required when KMS/HSM is available. The guide describes key ownership, rotation expectations, and what to do if KMS is temporarily unavailable.
- Scope boundaries: the guide focuses on control-plane state + critical configuration for deterministic redeploy. Data plane is treated as stateless; we document what config must be captured to redeploy deterministically (values files, model routing policies, etc.) and explicitly call out what is out of scope.

Operator flows documented (commands are examples; exact flags may vary by version):
- Create backup: rwi backup create --storage s3://<bucket>/<prefix> --encryption kms --manifest-out <path>
- Verify backup integrity: rwi backup verify --manifest <path>
- Restore: rwi restore apply --manifest <path> --target-cluster <context> --validate

Cross-links / references:
- Links to the internal runbooks (for Redwood SRE/Solutions) and to the public-facing pages as the source of truth for customers.
- Links to Velero integration guidance (resource include/exclude philosophy and coordination with Redwood snapshots), with explicit warnings to avoid duplicative/unsafe secret capture.

Why now:
- Support escalation SUP-18421 requested an official customer-facing runbook.
- On-prem customer restore issue SUP-18477 highlighted missing CRD/ordering guidance and pre-flight checks.

Follow-ups (not blocking this PR):
- Add an “RPO/RTO matrix” page once Product finalizes default targets per archetype.
- Add a short video walkthrough for restore validation and DR drills.

Screenshots:
- Included in docs preview (Netlify) as embedded images for the DR checklist pages.

Docs preview:
- Netlify deploy posted by CI (see checks).

title: Document retention controls + compliance reporting pack request flow (incl. FAQ updates)
preview:
Summary: This PR publishes initial external documentation for the Retention controls + compliance reporting pack workstream. It adds a retention controls overview (supported data classes, tier defaults, and how to configure), documents limitations and operational semantics (TTL vs purge, change history, verification), and updates the Security FAQ with customer-ready answers for security questionnaires.

Motivation: Enterprise security reviews (Dedicated/Private) are consistently asking for (a) configurable retention by data class and (b) a standardized evidence package for audit logs + retention configuration. Prior docs were a mix of internal Confluence and ad-hoc SE answers; this makes the baseline story consistent and reduces back-and-forth.

What’s included:
- New docs page: “Retention controls” covering supported data classes (request/response logs where enabled, audit logs, traces/metrics retention, and artifacts where applicable), what Redwood does by default by deployment tier, and how customers change retention (Console + API + Terraform for Private where supported).
- New docs page: “Retention controls limitations & semantics” clarifying: TTL enforcement is asynchronous; deletes are idempotent; retention controls apply to stored telemetry/logging data (not model weights); and short retention may reduce debugging history unless a documented break-glass exception is approved.
- Updated Security FAQ entries: “Do you support configurable retention?”, “Can you provide an audit log export?”, “What is included in the compliance reporting pack?”, “How do you handle legal hold / incident holds?”
- New section: “Requesting the compliance reporting pack” describing how to request the baseline package (via Support ticket or AE/SE), expected content (audit-log coverage summary, retention configuration export, data residency/region summary, RBAC/access report, evidence checklist), and typical turnaround for a specified time window.

Notes / wording constraints:
- Avoids making certification claims (no “SOC 2 certified” language); uses “aligned to SOC 2 / ISO-style controls” and “customer evidence pack” phrasing.
- Legal hold wording intentionally scoped: describes the concept and that holds are tracked/audited, but does not publish internal approval matrix details.
- Includes explicit caveats for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (e.g., some stores may be customer-managed in Private; retention enforcement depends on configured backing services and installed purge jobs).

Follow-ups (not in scope for this PR):
- Add screenshot annotations once the Console retention settings page is GA.
- Add a dedicated “Report pack format/versioning” page once the export format ADR lands in public docs.

Review checklist:
- [x] Security reviewed customer-facing terminology (audit logs vs request logs)
- [x] GRC reviewed evidence pack section headings
- [x] CS validated request flow and expectations
- [x] Docs build/link check green

title: Docs: add observability hooks reference for quickstart templates
preview:
Title: Docs: add observability hooks reference for quickstart templates

Context
We’re shipping production-minded RAG + agent quickstart templates that emit standardized OpenTelemetry signals plus Redwood-specific conventions. Support/SRE feedback has been consistent: we need a canonical reference page (1) defining the required spans/attributes/metrics/log fields, and (2) mapping those to Redwood Console dashboards so users can verify instrumentation quickly and understand what they’re looking at.

What this PR does
- Adds a new docs reference page: "Observability hooks (reference)" for Quickstart Templates.
- Documents required spans + span attributes (including what is REQUIRED vs RECOMMENDED).
- Documents required metrics (names, types, units, and allowed dimensions) and includes a “cardinality guardrails” section aligned with Observability/SRE guidance.
- Adds a “Mapping to Redwood Console” section that calls out which dashboards/panels use which signals (Templates Overview, RAG Latency Breakdown, Tool Failures, Retrieval Quality, Token Economics).
- Adds a small “How to validate locally” checklist (view trace tree, confirm metrics are flowing, confirm request-id correlation).

Non-goals
- This does not introduce new instrumentation in templates/SDKs; it’s documentation only.
- This does not document every possible OTel semantic convention; we focus on the minimum set needed to make templates supportable and dashboards functional.

Docs IA / links
- Linked from the Quickstart Templates landing page and the RAG+Agent walkthrough.
- Cross-linked from the Troubleshooting page ("No traces in Console?" and "Missing tool spans?").

New/updated content (high level)
1) Tracing
- Required span hierarchy (expected parent/child relationships):
  - http.server (or equivalent framework span)
    - redwood.llm.request
      - redwood.rag.retrieve (optional for non-RAG)
      - redwood.agent.iteration (0..N)
        - redwood.tool.<tool_name> (0..N)
- Required span attributes (REQUIRED):
  - redwood.request_id (from API response header when available; used for support correlation)
  - llm.system = "redwood" (or llm.provider = "redwood"; chosen term documented for consistency)
  - llm.model (pinned model identifier)
  - llm.operation ("chat" | "responses" | "embeddings" | "rerank")
  - http.response.status_code (for the client call span)
  - error.type (normalized error taxonomy keys)
- Recommended attributes (RECOMMENDED) with cardinality cautions:
  - redwood.deployment_mode ("hosted" | "dedicated" | "private")
  - redwood.region
  - redwood.template.name / redwood.template.version (for template-originated traffic tagging)
  - tool.name (low-cardinality) and tool.result (DO NOT log raw output)
- Redaction rules:
  - Explicitly prohibit recording prompt/user content as span attributes.
  - Recommend hashing document IDs if they must be included.
  - Provide examples of safe logging fields.

2) Metrics
- Required metrics (names, types, and units):
  - redwood.llm.latency_ms (histogram)
  - redwood.llm.tokens.input (counter)
  - redwood.llm.tokens.output (counter)
  - redwood.llm.requests (counter)
  - redwood.llm.errors (counter)
  - redwood.rag.retrieval.latency_ms (histogram)
  - redwood.rag.retrieval.results_count (histogram)
  - redwood.tool.calls (counter)
  - redwood.tool.errors (counter)
- Required metric dimensions (allowed list):
  - llm.model, llm.operation, redwood.template.name, redwood.template.version
- Forbidden/high-risk dimensions (explicit):
  - user_id, session_id, request_id, full URL paths, document titles, arbitrary tool args

3) Logs
- Minimal structured log fields to align with support workflows:
  - level, message, timestamp
  - redwood.request_id
  - error.type, error.message (redacted)
  - llm.model, llm.operation
  - tool.name (if relevant)

4) Mapping to Redwood Console
- Dashboard: Templates Overview
  - Uses redwood.template.name/version + redwood.llm.requests + redwood.llm.latency_ms
- Dashboard: Token Economics
  - Uses redwood.llm.tokens.input/output + latency histograms for cost/latency analysis
- Dashboard: Tool Failures
  - Uses redwood.tool.errors + tool spans with error.type/tool.name
- Dashboard: RAG Latency Breakdown
  - Uses redwood.rag.retrieval.latency_ms span timing + llm request span timing

Why this matters
- Reduces "is it instrumented correctly?" support load.
- Makes template dashboards immediately actionable.
- Prevents accidental high-cardinality tags and prompt/PII leakage.

Implementation notes
- Content is aligned with internal standards discussed with Allison/Logan (span naming + attribute set) and SRE feedback (cardinality guardrails, redaction language).
- Added a small admonition block where we previously saw proxy issues with opt-in telemetry headers; the reference clarifies that template tagging is optional and must not include user data.

Screenshots
- N/A (docs-only). Included example trace tree and metric table inline.

Testing
- `pnpm lint`
- `pnpm test:links`
- `pnpm build`

Follow-ups
- After merge, we should link this page from the SDK instrumentation guide (Wei is tracking separately) and ensure the observability pack dashboard names match the final docs strings.


Commits
1) docs(quickstarts): add observability hooks reference page
2) docs(quickstarts): link reference from templates landing + walkthrough
3) docs(observability): add metric tables + cardinality guidance
4) chore(ci): fix link checker failures for new anchors

Changed files / areas
- docs/quickstarts/templates/observability-hooks-reference.md (new)
- docs/quickstarts/templates/_index.md (add reference link)
- docs/quickstarts/templates/rag-agent-walkthrough.md (add “Verify observability” section + link)
- docs/quickstarts/templates/troubleshooting.md (add cross-links to reference)
- docs/partials/otel-attribute-table.md (new reusable table partial)
- docs/partials/otel-metric-table.md (new reusable table partial)
- docs/nav.yml (add page to Quickstart Templates section)

Illustrative snippet (from observability-hooks-reference.md)
Span names (required): http.server -> redwood.llm.request -> (optional) redwood.rag.retrieve -> (optional) redwood.agent.iteration -> (optional) redwood.tool.<tool_name>. Required attributes on redwood.llm.request: llm.model, llm.operation, redwood.request_id (when available), llm.system="redwood".

Review conversation (highlights)
- Allison Grant: "Please call out allowed metric dimensions explicitly and add a warning about request_id/user_id as labels. Also clarify span attribute redaction (no prompts)." Author (Elliot): "Added ‘Allowed dimensions’ list, ‘Forbidden dimensions’ list, and a dedicated redaction section with examples. Updated wording to match quickstart tracing conventions." (resolved)
- Logan Wright: "Mapping to Console: can we be precise about which panels use template tags vs generic OTel? Also note that template tags are opt-in." Author: "Updated mapping section to indicate dashboard filters use `redwood.template.*` tags when present, otherwise dashboards fall back to model/operation aggregates. Added opt-in note + privacy language." (resolved)
- Christine Wong: "Local validation checklist is good. Add a note that missing traces is often collector endpoint/auth, and link to troubleshooting." Author: "Added explicit link to troubleshooting and a short callout for collector auth/endpoints." (resolved)
- Monica Patel: "Please ensure attribute naming matches what templates actually emit (redwood.template.version vs template_version)." Author: "Aligned to template naming convention: `redwood.template.name` and `redwood.template.version`." (resolved)
- Sergio Costa: "Metric names should be consistent with dashboard pack. I think retrieval metric is `redwood.rag.retrieval.latency_ms` not `redwood.rag.retrieve.latency_ms`." Author: "Updated metric names and added a short ‘naming alignment’ note." (resolved)

CI
- docs-build (pass)
- lint (pass)
- link-check (initial fail: broken anchor to troubleshooting section; fixed in commit 4; rerun pass)

Merge
- Merged via squash on 2025-02-28 by Elliot Price after approvals from Allison Grant and Christine Wong.

title: Docs: Data residency region pinning (Hosted) — configuration, limitations, and troubleshooting
preview: This PR adds public documentation for Hosted data residency enforcement via region pinning. It covers: (1) what region pinning guarantees, (2) how to configure allowed regions + primary region in the Console and via Admin API (where available), (3) enforcement behavior (routing + fallback constraints) and common failure modes, (4) the public error contract for residency violations (HTTP status, error_code, request_id, help_url), and (5) troubleshooting steps and support-ready guidance. The content is aligned with the current gateway contract (409 on deterministic policy block) and clarifies limitations/non-goals (no automatic multi-region replication; some non-content account metadata may be processed outside the pinned region). It also includes copy for streaming vs non-streaming behavior and points users to audit log + blocked-request views in Console. Internal-only implementation details are intentionally omitted; this is customer-facing guidance only. Docs note: terminology standardized to “Allowed regions” and “Primary region” per Product guidance; avoids “geo-fencing” language.

title: Internal docs: add Unit Economics dashboard hub + link runbooks/definitions
preview: This PR updates the internal docs site to include a single “Unit Economics Dashboard” hub page and fixes/standardizes links to the canonical Confluence sources for definitions, SLOs, and oncall procedures. The goal is to reduce ping-pong across Slack threads and PR descriptions by having one stable entry point that Finance/Product/Eng can bookmark. The hub also documents access control expectations (feature flag + RBAC) and explicitly calls out v1 limitations so the metric isn’t over-interpreted. Changes include: (1) new internal docs page that links to the PRD, UX spec, metric dictionary, ADR for cost attribution, and Finance methodology note; (2) links to the SLO page and the oncall runbook (investigation + backfill) plus a short “how to validate freshness/completeness” checklist; (3) updated navigation entry under Observability → Cost & Usage; (4) replaced stale/notionally-correct but broken URLs referenced from older dashboard docs; (5) added a short section on data quality/confidence indicators and what to do when the dashboard disagrees with billing (points to reconciliation report/runbook). No product behavior changes; docs-only.

title: Docs: RBAC v2 concepts, permission catalog, role templates, and migration notes
preview:
Summary
Adds first-pass public documentation for RBAC v2 across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. This PR introduces: (1) a conceptual overview of RBAC v2 primitives (permissions, roles, bindings, scopes), (2) a permission catalog reference page (with naming conventions and deprecation/versioning notes), (3) built-in role templates with recommended assignments, and (4) migration notes from RBAC v1/coarse roles including dual-read behavior and troubleshooting for permission denied.

Motivation / context
Enterprise customers (Dedicated + Private) are asking for least-privilege guidance and auditability artifacts. Support/Solutions also need a stable reference for: “what does this permission do?”, “which role should I assign?”, and “why am I denied?”. The docs in this PR align to the RBAC v2 enforcement + audit decision logging work landing in platform services.

What’s included
- New docs page: /security/rbac-v2/overview
  - Definitions: Permission, Role, Role Binding, Subject (user/service principal), Scope (org/project/environment/cluster)
  - Enforcement model: deny-by-default, gateway + service checks, consistent 403 semantics
  - Auditability: authz decision context fields (subject/action/resource scope/policy version/decision/denial reason)
- New docs page: /security/rbac-v2/permissions
  - Permission string format + namespacing (redwood.<domain>.<resource>.<action>)
  - Stability + deprecation policy (no silent semantic changes; documented replacements)
  - “Sensitive operations” callouts (key rotation, secret operations, role changes, routing policy edits, audit export)
- New docs page: /security/rbac-v2/role-templates
  - Built-in templates (Org Admin, Security Admin, Billing Admin, Read-only Auditor, Deployment Operator)
  - Guidance for separation of duties (Security Admin ≠ Billing Admin by default)
  - Scope examples for Dedicated/Private (cluster-scoped operator; environment-scoped auditor)
- New docs page: /security/rbac-v2/migration
  - Dual-read/feature flag rollout explanation
  - Expected behavior during transition (v1 roles mapped to v2 templates; denies surfaced with reason codes)
  - Troubleshooting: interpreting denial reason codes; when to escalate; how to request temporary elevation

Non-goals
- This does not document a customer-exposed policy language/ABAC.
- This does not publish internal-only permissions or break-glass runbooks in full; we link to a high-level “Emergency access” section and keep operational steps internal.

Notable details / decisions
- Permission catalog is presented as a curated reference with examples + categories rather than a full dump of every internal action. The page includes a “How to find the exact permission” section pointing to Console Permission Viewer (coming soon) and API error details when debug headers are enabled for admins.
- Migration page explicitly calls out that some previously implicit capabilities may require explicit grants under v2 (deny-by-default).

Screenshots
- N/A (text-only docs). I verified local rendering and navigation.

Checklist
- [x] Content reviewed with Security Eng + PM
- [x] Link checker passes (no broken internal anchors)
- [x] Adds redirect stubs for old RBAC v1 doc URLs
- [x] Notes on audit log fields match current schema naming

Follow-ups
- Add a generated “permissions table” section once the permission registry stabilizes (to avoid churn). Proposed approach: nightly job to export a versioned markdown artifact from the registry used by services.
- Add Console screenshots for role editor and permission viewer once DES-914 lands in product UI.

title: Introduce RAG authoring UX walkthrough and compact step guides
preview:
Motivation: teams building retrieval-augmented workflows repeatedly ask for concise authoring guidance and a lightweight in-doc walkthrough they can fork into their apps. This PR adds a compact UX-centered walkthrough for RAG authoring, short step guides targeted at SDK users, and a small interactive tutorial embedded in the docs site. The goal is to reduce onboarding time for engineers integrating retrieval + tool-calling flows and to provide reproducible local dev steps and CI smoke checks.

What this PR contains: - a new docs section `docs/rag/ux-walkthrough.md` with a step-by-step narrative of common authoring flows (indexing, prompt scaffolding, tool binding, and retry strategies); - `docs/rag/compact-steps.md`: a 1-page quick checklist for productionizing a RAG endpoint with samples for Python/TS; - `docs/snippets/ux-templates/` tiny prompt templates plus a short JSON schema for tool contracts; - a short interactive tutorial harness integrated into the docs site (static/ux-walkthrough/index.html + a tiny client shim) that can be toggled by readers; - small CI smoke tests (ci/docs-smoke.yaml) which validate build + tutorial boot; - site navigation changes and search tags.

Checklist: - [x] Add walkthrough markdown and compact guides - [x] Add small interactive static example and client shim - [x] Add CI smoke job to ensure tutorial loads in site build - [x] Add minimal screenshots and alt text for accessibility - [x] Request reviews from Docs and SDK owners

Compatibility and scope: This is documentation and static site work only; no runtime or API surface changes. The interactive tutorial uses the existing local mock harness (already in repo) and is gated behind the docs build so it does not affect production infra.

How to review: Read the walkthrough and compact steps for clarity and completeness. Run the site locally via `make site` then navigate to /rag/ux-walkthrough and toggle the embedded tutorial. CI smoke is lightweight and should be run in the docs pipeline.

Related work: references ENG-4127 for onboarding telemetry improvements and PM-923 for the docs initiative. Linked support ticket SUP-341 contains customer requests that motivated the UX examples.

Notes: kept the prose compact on purpose — the walkthrough emphasizes actionable checklists and copy-paste snippets. Screenshots are optimized and intentionally low-res to keep site bundle size small.
